vocabWords.definition_7_10: Stretches of sand or pebbles along the shoreline where the water meets the land.
vocabWords.exampleSentence: The children built sandcastles on the beaches during their summer vacation.

vocabWords.definition_4_7: A state in the northeastern part of America.
vocabWords.definition_7_10: One of the first American states, located in New England where the Pilgrims landed.
vocabWords.exampleSentence: Massachusetts is famous for its beautiful fall leaves and historic sites.

pages.label: Page 6
pages.text: Before we go ashore, all the men gather to sign an important paper called the Mayflower Compact. Papa explains that it says we will make fair laws together and work as one community. Even though we are far from England, we promise to govern ourselves fairly. Governor John Carver and William Bradford are among those who sign their names with careful handwriting. This document will help us build our new colony in peace.
pages.scene: Men in Puritan clothing (black coats, white collars, tall hats) gathered around a wooden table on the ship's deck. A document (the Mayflower Compact) is spread on the table with a quill pen and inkwell. Governor Carver, a middle-aged man with a beard, is signing. Other men wait their turn, looking serious and determined.

vocabWords.definition_4_7: An important agreement or promise written on paper.
vocabWords.definition_7_10: A formal written agreement between people that creates rules everyone promises to follow.
vocabWords.exampleSentence: The two countries signed a compact to share water from the river peacefully.

vocabWords.definition_4_7: A group of people who live together in one place.
vocabWords.definition_7_10: A group of people who share the same area and work together for their common good.
vocabWords.exampleSentence: The small community came together to help rebuild the school after the storm.

vocabWords.definition_4_7: To make rules and decisions for a group of people.
vocabWords.definition_7_10: To control and guide a group by making laws and important decisions for everyone.
vocabWords.exampleSentence: The student council will govern the new rules for recess activities at school.

vocabWords.definition_4_7: An important paper with writing that proves something.
vocabWords.definition_7_10: An official written paper that contains important information or creates legal agreements.
vocabWords.exampleSentence: The birth certificate is an important document that proves when you were born.

pages.label: Page 7
pages.text: The men row small boats to shore to explore our new home. When they return, they tell us about forests full of tall pine trees, fresh water streams, and clearings where we can build houses. But winter is coming fast, and the air grows colder each day. We decide to build our settlement on a hill near a large rock by the shore. Some people start calling it Plymouth Rock, like our old home in England.
pages.scene: Small wooden rowboats with Pilgrims rowing toward a wooded shore. Large pine and oak trees with some autumn leaves still clinging to branches. A prominent boulder (Plymouth Rock) visible near the waterline. The Mayflower anchored in the harbor behind them. Cold, gray November sky.

vocabWords.definition_4_7: To look around and discover new places or things.
vocabWords.definition_7_10: To travel through unfamiliar territory to learn about and discover what's there.
vocabWords.exampleSentence: The children love to explore the woods behind their house on weekends.

vocabWords.definition_4_7: Small rivers of fresh water that flow through the land.
vocabWords.definition_7_10: Narrow flows of fresh water that move naturally through the countryside toward larger rivers.
vocabWords.exampleSentence: The deer came to drink from the clear mountain streams every morning.

vocabWords.definition_4_7: Open spaces in the forest where no trees grow.
vocabWords.definition_7_10: Areas in the woods where trees have been removed, creating open spaces perfect for building.
vocabWords.exampleSentence: The family found a sunny clearing in the forest that was perfect for their picnic.

vocabWords.definition_4_7: A place where people build homes and start living together.
vocabWords.definition_7_10: A new community where people establish homes and create a place to live permanently.
vocabWords.exampleSentence: The pioneers worked together to build their settlement near the fertile valley.

pages.label: Page 8
pages.text: Winter arrives with bitter cold and deep snow. Many people become sick, and we do not have enough food or warm shelter. Our first homes are just holes dug into the hillside covered with branches and dirt. The wind cuts through our thin clothes like knives. By spring, almost half of our people have died, including my dear mother. Papa and I hold each other tight and pray for better days to come.
pages.scene: A harsh winter scene showing crude shelters - dugout homes in a snowy hillside covered with branches and bark. Smoke rising from small chimneys. Pilgrims in heavy cloaks walking through deep snow. Pine trees heavy with snow, gray winter sky. The scene shows the hardship but also determination of the colonists.

vocabWords.definition_4_7: Very cold and harsh, making you feel uncomfortable.
vocabWords.definition_7_10: Extremely cold and unpleasant, causing pain and discomfort from the freezing temperature.
vocabWords.exampleSentence: The bitter wind made the children hurry inside to warm up by the fire.

vocabWords.definition_4_7: A safe place that protects you from bad weather.
vocabWords.definition_7_10: A structure or building that provides protection from wind, rain, cold, and other dangers.
vocabWords.exampleSentence: The hikers found shelter in a cave when the thunderstorm began.

vocabWords.definition_4_7: The sloping side of a hill or mountain.
vocabWords.definition_7_10: The slanted surface of a hill, often used for building homes or farming crops.
vocabWords.exampleSentence: The sheep grazed peacefully on the green hillside above the valley.

pages.label: Page 9
pages.text: When spring arrives, a Native American man named Squanto comes to our village. He speaks English, which amazes us! He was captured years ago but learned our language in England before finding his way home. Squanto teaches us how to plant corn, beans, and squash together in small hills. He shows us where to catch fish and gather shellfish. Without his help, we surely would have starved.
pages.scene: A Native American man (Squanto) in traditional leather clothing with fringe, demonstrating planting techniques to Pilgrims in a cleared field. Small mounds of earth with corn, beans, and squash planted together. Pilgrims in their work clothes (brown and gray garments, rolled sleeves) watching and learning. Spring setting with budding trees.

vocabWords.definition_4_7: Caught and taken away from home against your will.
vocabWords.definition_7_10: Seized and held prisoner, usually taken far from your family and homeland by force.
vocabWords.exampleSentence: The bird was captured by scientists who wanted to study its migration patterns.

vocabWords.definition_4_7: Surprises you so much that you can hardly believe it.
vocabWords.definition_7_10: Fills you with wonder and astonishment because something seems almost impossible to believe.
vocabWords.exampleSentence: The magician's trick amazes the audience every time he performs it.

vocabWords.definition_4_7: A vegetable that grows on vines and has thick skin.
vocabWords.definition_7_10: A large vegetable with hard skin that grows on the ground and can be cooked many ways.
vocabWords.exampleSentence: The farmer grew yellow squash in his garden that became delicious soup.

vocabWords.definition_4_7: Sea animals with hard shells that you can eat.
vocabWords.definition_7_10: Ocean creatures like clams, crabs, and lobsters that have protective hard shells around their bodies.
vocabWords.exampleSentence: The restaurant served fresh shellfish caught by local fishermen that morning.

pages.label: Page 10
pages.text: Chief Massasoit and many Wampanoag people help us survive in this new land. They bring deer meat and wild turkeys to share with us. After our first successful harvest in autumn 1621, we invite them to a great feast of thanksgiving. For three days we eat, play games, and give thanks to God together. I am twelve now, and though I miss Mama terribly, I am grateful for our new friends and our new home in Plymouth.
pages.scene: A harvest feast scene with long wooden tables outdoors. Pilgrims in their best clothes (still simple and dark) sitting alongside Wampanoag people in traditional dress with feathers and leather garments. Roasted turkey, corn, squash, and other foods on the tables. Children playing nearby. Autumn trees with colorful leaves in background.

vocabWords.definition_4_7: The Native American people who lived in Massachusetts first.
vocabWords.definition_7_10: The indigenous tribe that lived in the Massachusetts area long before the Pilgrims arrived.
vocabWords.exampleSentence: The Wampanoag people had lived in the region for thousands of years before Europeans came.

vocabWords.definition_4_7: Large birds with feathers that can be cooked and eaten.
vocabWords.definition_7_10: Big birds with colorful feathers that live in the wild and provide meat for meals.
vocabWords.exampleSentence: Wild turkeys roamed through the forest searching for acorns and seeds to eat.

vocabWords.definition_4_7: The time when you gather crops you grew all year.
vocabWords.definition_7_10: The season when farmers collect the fruits and vegetables they planted and cared for all year.
vocabWords.exampleSentence: The farmers worked hard during harvest time to gather all their corn and pumpkins.

vocabWords.definition_4_7: A special time to say thank you for good things.
vocabWords.definition_7_10: A celebration where people gather to express gratitude for their blessings and good fortune.
vocabWords.exampleSentence: The family held a thanksgiving dinner to celebrate their new baby's safe arrival.

vocabWords.definition_4_7: Feeling happy and thankful for something good that happened.
vocabWords.definition_7_10: Feeling deep appreciation and thankfulness for kindness or good things in your life.
vocabWords.exampleSentence: She felt grateful for her teacher's extra help with the difficult math problems.

comprehensionQuestions.question: How long did Mary and the other Pilgrims spend sailing across the ocean?

options.text: Sixty-six days
Three months
One hundred days

comprehensionQuestions.question: Why do you think Squanto was so important to the Pilgrims' survival?

options.text: He could speak English and taught them how to grow food
He gave them a place to live in his village
He helped them build the Mayflower ship

comprehensionQuestions.question: How do you think Mary felt when she watched England disappear from the porthole?

options.text: Scared and sad to leave everything familiar behind
Excited but nervous about the unknown adventure ahead
Worried about what their new home would be like

predictionPause.question: Winter has been so hard that many people have died, including Mary's mother. What do you think will happen when spring comes?

options.text: The surviving Pilgrims will get help from local Native Americans
They will decide to sail back to England on the Mayflower
More ships will arrive from England with supplies and people

h3.title: Amelia Earhart's Big Flight
h3.topic: Amelia Earhart, the first woman to fly solo across the Atlantic Ocean in 1932. She flew from Newfoundland to Northern Ireland in about 15 hours. She inspired millions of women and girls to pursue their dreams. She disappeared in 1937 while attempting to fly around the world.
h3.keyFacts: Born 1897 in Kansas, first woman solo transatlantic flight May 1932, flew a Lockheed Vega, landed in a field in Northern Ireland, also set altitude records, disappeared July 1937 over the Pacific during round-the-world attempt

pages.label: Page 1
pages.text: In 1897, a baby girl named Amelia was born in Kansas. As she grew up, little Amelia loved to climb trees and explore. She built a roller coaster in her backyard and wasn't afraid of anything! Even as a young girl, Amelia dreamed of big adventures.
pages.scene: A young girl with short blonde bob hair in 1900s dress climbing a tall oak tree in rural Kansas, with a wooden farmhouse and endless prairie in the background

vocabWords.definition_4_7: To look around and discover new places or things.
vocabWords.definition_7_10: To travel through unfamiliar areas with curiosity, searching for new discoveries and experiences.
vocabWords.exampleSentence: The children wanted to explore the mysterious cave behind the waterfall.

vocabWords.definition_4_7: A fun ride that goes up and down on tracks.
vocabWords.definition_7_10: An exciting ride with cars that roll along curved tracks, going fast over hills and valleys.
vocabWords.exampleSentence: The wooden roller coaster at the amusement park was thrilling and bumpy.

vocabWords.definition_4_7: Exciting and sometimes dangerous trips or experiences.
vocabWords.definition_7_10: Thrilling journeys or experiences that involve some risk and lead to amazing discoveries.
vocabWords.exampleSentence: The pirates sailed across the ocean searching for treasure and new adventures.

pages.label: Page 2
pages.text: When Amelia grew up, she saw her first airplane at a fair. The pilot took her high up in the sky! She felt the wind rushing past and saw the world spread out like a patchwork quilt below. 'I want to fly!' she said.
pages.scene: A 1920s biplane soaring over farmland, with a young woman with short blonde hair in the passenger seat wearing leather flying goggles and jacket, looking amazed at the view below

vocabWords.definition_4_7: A machine with wings that flies people through the sky.
vocabWords.definition_7_10: A powered flying vehicle with fixed wings that carries passengers or cargo through the air.
vocabWords.exampleSentence: The big airplane carried hundreds of people to faraway countries.

vocabWords.definition_4_7: A person who flies and controls an airplane.
vocabWords.definition_7_10: A skilled person who operates aircraft controls and navigates planes safely through the sky.
vocabWords.exampleSentence: The pilot announced that they would land in twenty minutes.

vocabWords.definition_4_7: Different colored pieces sewn together to make a pattern.
vocabWords.definition_7_10: A design made from many different colored pieces stitched together, creating a beautiful mosaic pattern.
vocabWords.exampleSentence: Grandmother made a warm patchwork blanket from old fabric scraps.

pages.label: Page 3
pages.text: Amelia learned to fly her own airplane. She worked hard, practicing takeoffs and landings. She wore leather pants, a jacket, and flying goggles. Many people thought only men should be pilots, but Amelia proved them wrong!
pages.scene: Amelia Earhart in 1920s aviation clothing standing next to a small biplane, adjusting her leather flying goggles, with other male pilots watching in the background at an airfield

vocabWords.definition_4_7: Doing something over and over to get better at it.
vocabWords.definition_7_10: Repeating an activity many times to improve your skills and become more confident doing it.
vocabWords.exampleSentence: Maria spent hours practicing piano scales before her recital.

vocabWords.definition_4_7: When an airplane leaves the ground and starts flying.
vocabWords.definition_7_10: The moment when an aircraft accelerates down the runway and lifts into the air to begin flight.
vocabWords.exampleSentence: The airport was busy with takeoffs and landings all day long.

vocabWords.definition_4_7: Special glasses that protect your eyes from wind and dirt.
vocabWords.definition_7_10: Protective eyewear with a tight seal around the eyes to shield them from wind, dust, or water.
vocabWords.exampleSentence: The swimmer wore goggles to see clearly underwater.

pages.label: Page 4
pages.text: In May 1932, Amelia decided to do something no woman had ever done before. She would fly all by herself across the Atlantic Ocean! Her bright red Lockheed Vega airplane was loaded with extra fuel tanks for the long journey.
pages.scene: A bright red Lockheed Vega aircraft on an airfield in Newfoundland, with Amelia Earhart in her flight suit checking equipment, and mechanics adding fuel to the plane

vocabWords.definition_4_7: A huge ocean between America and Europe.
vocabWords.definition_7_10: The massive body of water that separates North America from Europe and Africa, spanning thousands of miles.
vocabWords.exampleSentence: Ships cross the Atlantic Ocean to trade goods between different countries.

vocabWords.definition_4_7: The name of a company that makes airplanes.
vocabWords.definition_7_10: A famous American aircraft manufacturing company known for building high-quality planes and spacecraft.
vocabWords.exampleSentence: The museum displayed a vintage Lockheed fighter jet from World War Two.

vocabWords.definition_4_7: Special liquid that makes engines run and gives them power.
vocabWords.definition_7_10: A combustible substance like gasoline that provides energy to power engines and make vehicles move.
vocabWords.exampleSentence: Dad stopped at the gas station to put fuel in our car.

pages.label: Page 5
pages.text: Early in the morning, Amelia climbed into her cockpit. The engine roared to life, and she rolled down the runway in Newfoundland, Canada. Up, up, up she went into the cloudy sky, heading east over the vast, dark Atlantic Ocean.
pages.scene: The red Lockheed Vega taking off from a grass runway in Newfoundland at dawn, with dramatic clouds and the Atlantic Ocean stretching endlessly ahead

vocabWords.definition_4_7: The special room inside an airplane where the pilot sits.
vocabWords.definition_7_10: The enclosed area at the front of an aircraft where the pilot controls all the instruments and steering.
vocabWords.exampleSentence: The cockpit had dozens of buttons, switches, and glowing screens.

vocabWords.definition_4_7: Made a very loud, powerful sound like a lion.
vocabWords.definition_7_10: Produced a deep, thunderous noise that rumbled powerfully and could be heard from far away.
vocabWords.exampleSentence: The motorcycle roared down the street, waking up the whole neighborhood.

vocabWords.definition_4_7: A long, flat road where airplanes take off and land.
vocabWords.definition_7_10: A specially paved strip of ground at an airport designed for aircraft to accelerate for takeoff or slow down after landing.
vocabWords.exampleSentence: The runway was cleared of snow so planes could land safely.

pages.label: Page 6
pages.text: For fifteen long hours, Amelia flew through clouds and storms. Ice formed on her wings. Her compass spun around and around, and she got lost! But brave Amelia kept flying, following the stars and trusting her skills.
pages.scene: Inside the cramped cockpit of the Lockheed Vega at night, showing Amelia's hands gripping the controls, ice crystals on the windscreen, and stars visible through breaks in storm clouds

vocabWords.definition_4_7: A tool with a needle that points north to help you find directions.
vocabWords.definition_7_10: A navigation instrument with a magnetic needle that always points toward magnetic north to guide travelers.
vocabWords.exampleSentence: The hikers used a compass to find their way through the dense forest.

vocabWords.definition_4_7: Bad weather with strong wind, rain, and sometimes thunder.
vocabWords.definition_7_10: Violent weather systems with powerful winds, heavy precipitation, and sometimes lightning that can be dangerous.
vocabWords.exampleSentence: The sailors waited in the harbor until the storms passed over the ocean.

vocabWords.definition_4_7: Believing in something or someone without being scared.
vocabWords.definition_7_10: Having confidence and faith in someone or something, believing they will be reliable and helpful.
vocabWords.exampleSentence: The little girl was trusting her big brother to catch her when she jumped.

pages.label: Page 7
pages.text: Finally, Amelia saw green fields below. She was running out of fuel, so she brought her plane down in a grassy pasture in Northern Ireland. Cows scattered as she landed with a bumpy thump!
pages.scene: The red Lockheed Vega landing in a green Irish pasture with rolling hills, stone walls, and surprised cows running away, while curious farmers watch from nearby

vocabWords.definition_4_7: A grassy field where farm animals eat and live.
vocabWords.definition_7_10: An open area of grassland where livestock like cows and sheep graze and roam freely.
vocabWords.exampleSentence: The horses ran happily through the green pasture behind the barn.

vocabWords.definition_4_7: Ran away quickly in different directions when something scared them.
vocabWords.definition_7_10: Moved apart rapidly and spread out in various directions, usually because of surprise or fear.
vocabWords.exampleSentence: The pigeons scattered when the dog barked loudly in the park.

vocabWords.definition_4_7: A loud, heavy sound when something hits the ground hard.
vocabWords.definition_7_10: A deep, dull sound produced when a heavy object strikes a surface with considerable force.
vocabWords.exampleSentence: There was a loud thump when the heavy box fell off the truck.

pages.label: Page 8
pages.text: Amelia had done it! She was the first woman to fly solo across the Atlantic Ocean. Newspapers around the world told her story. Girls everywhere learned that they could chase their dreams too, just like Amelia.
pages.scene: Newspaper stands in 1930s city streets with headlines about Amelia's flight, while excited children and adults gather around reading the news, with young girls looking inspired

vocabWords.definition_4_7: All by yourself, without any help from other people.
vocabWords.definition_7_10: Done alone without assistance or companionship from others, showing independence and self-reliance.
vocabWords.exampleSentence: The young musician performed a solo piano piece at the talent show.

vocabWords.definition_4_7: Printed papers with news stories and information that people read.
vocabWords.definition_7_10: Publications printed on paper that contain current events, articles, and information distributed to the public daily.
vocabWords.exampleSentence: Grandfather read three different newspapers every morning with his coffee.

pages.label: Page 9
pages.text: Amelia kept flying and setting records. She flew higher than any woman before her and inspired millions of people. In 1937, she tried to fly all the way around the world in her silver airplane.
pages.scene: Amelia Earhart standing confidently next to her silver Lockheed Electra aircraft, with a world map showing her planned route displayed on an easel nearby, surrounded by reporters and cameras

vocabWords.definition_4_7: Amazing achievements that no one else has done before.
vocabWords.definition_7_10: Outstanding accomplishments or performances that surpass what anyone has previously achieved in that area.
vocabWords.exampleSentence: The swimmer broke three world records at the Olympic games.

vocabWords.definition_4_7: Made other people want to try hard and do great things too.
vocabWords.definition_7_10: Motivated and encouraged others to pursue their dreams and achieve remarkable things through positive example.
vocabWords.exampleSentence: The teacher inspired her students to become scientists and inventors.

vocabWords.definition_4_7: A really, really big number of people or things.
vocabWords.definition_7_10: An extremely large quantity, specifically referring to thousands of thousands of something.
vocabWords.exampleSentence: Millions of stars twinkled in the clear night sky above the desert.

pages.label: Page 10
pages.text: During that long journey around the world, Amelia's airplane disappeared over the Pacific Ocean, and she was never found. But her brave spirit lives on, inspiring new generations of pilots and dreamers to reach for the sky.
pages.scene: A peaceful sky at sunset with clouds forming the silhouette of an airplane, while below, modern children look up at the sky with wonder, some holding toy planes

vocabWords.definition_4_7: Went away and could not be found anywhere.
vocabWords.definition_7_10: Vanished completely from view and could not be located despite extensive searching efforts.
vocabWords.exampleSentence: The magician's rabbit disappeared from the hat and was nowhere to be seen.

vocabWords.definition_4_7: The biggest ocean in the world, between America and Asia.
vocabWords.definition_7_10: The largest ocean on Earth, stretching between the Americas and Asia, covering about one-third of the planet.
vocabWords.exampleSentence: Whales migrate across the vast Pacific Ocean every year.

vocabWords.definition_4_7: Groups of people who are born around the same time.
vocabWords.definition_7_10: Different age groups of people, like grandparents, parents, and children, who grow up in different time periods.
vocabWords.exampleSentence: Three generations of the family gathered for the special birthday celebration.

comprehensionQuestions.question: What did Amelia build in her backyard when she was little?

options.text: A roller coaster
A tree house
A swimming pool

comprehensionQuestions.question: Why do you think Amelia's compass was spinning around during her flight?

options.text: The storm was making it not work properly
She forgot to turn it on
The airplane was flying upside down

comprehensionQuestions.question: How would you feel if you were trying to do something that no one like you had ever done before?

options.text: Scared but excited to try something new
Proud to be the first one to do it
Nervous but determined to succeed

predictionPause.question: Amelia is lost over the ocean with ice on her wings! What do you think will happen next?

options.text: She'll find land and make it safely
She'll have to turn back home
Another pilot will come help her

h4.title: The Great Wall of China
h4.topic: The Great Wall of China, built over many centuries to protect China from invasions. Construction began as early as the 7th century BC, with major work under Emperor Qin Shi Huang around 221 BC. The wall stretches over 13,000 miles across mountains, deserts, and plains.
h4.keyFacts: Over 13,000 miles long, built over 2000+ years, many dynasties contributed, Emperor Qin Shi Huang unified earlier walls around 221 BC, made of stone brick tamped earth and wood, watchtowers every few hundred meters, visible from hilltops but NOT from space contrary to myth, UNESCO World Heritage Site

pages.label: Page 1
pages.text: Little Mei lived in a mountain village during the Qin Dynasty, over 2,000 years ago. Every morning, she watched workers carrying heavy stones and wooden beams up the steep hillside. 'What are they building, Grandmother?' Mei asked. Her grandmother smiled and pointed to the growing wall stretching across the peaks.
pages.scene: A young Chinese girl in simple Han-era clothing standing with her elderly grandmother outside a traditional village home. Workers in ancient Chinese clothing carry stones and wooden beams up a mountain path. The early stages of the Great Wall are visible on distant peaks.

vocabWords.definition_4_7: A family of rulers who control a country for many years.
vocabWords.definition_7_10: A powerful family whose members rule a country one after another for many generations.
vocabWords.exampleSentence: The royal dynasty passed the crown from father to son for hundreds of years.

vocabWords.definition_4_7: Long thick pieces of wood used to build things.
vocabWords.definition_7_10: Strong wooden pieces that support buildings and structures, like the backbone of a house.
vocabWords.exampleSentence: The carpenter used heavy beams to hold up the barn's roof.

vocabWords.definition_4_7: A small town where not very many people live.
vocabWords.definition_7_10: A small community in the countryside where families know each other and live simply.
vocabWords.exampleSentence: The fishing village had only twelve houses near the ocean.

vocabWords.definition_4_7: Very hard to climb up because it goes straight up.
vocabWords.definition_7_10: Rising sharply at a difficult angle that makes climbing or walking very challenging.
vocabWords.exampleSentence: The steep stairs made her legs tired after climbing to the top.

pages.label: Page 2
pages.text: Grandmother's eyes twinkled as she began her story. 'Long ago, different kingdoms built their own walls to protect their lands. Some were made of tamped earth, others of wood and stone. Emperor Qin Shi Huang had a grand idea - to connect all these walls into one great barrier!'
pages.scene: Grandmother gesturing toward the horizon while Mei listens intently. In the background, a misty landscape shows different sections of ancient walls made of various materials - earth, wood, and stone - stretching across hills and valleys.

vocabWords.definition_4_7: Countries that are ruled by kings or queens.
vocabWords.definition_7_10: Lands governed by royal families, each with their own laws and armies to protect their people.
vocabWords.exampleSentence: The two kingdoms decided to become friends instead of fighting each other.

vocabWords.definition_4_7: Packed down hard and tight by hitting or pressing.
vocabWords.definition_7_10: Pressed down firmly and repeatedly until the material becomes solid and strong like concrete.
vocabWords.exampleSentence: Dad tamped the dirt in the garden so the seeds wouldn't wash away.

vocabWords.definition_4_7: The most powerful ruler of a very big country.
vocabWords.definition_7_10: A supreme leader who controls many kingdoms and has more power than regular kings or queens.
vocabWords.exampleSentence: The emperor wore golden robes during the important ceremony at his palace.

vocabWords.definition_4_7: Something that blocks or stops things from getting through.
vocabWords.definition_7_10: A wall or fence built to keep unwanted people or things from entering a protected area.
vocabWords.exampleSentence: The fence was a barrier that kept the rabbits out of our vegetable garden.

pages.label: Page 3
pages.text: The next day, Mei climbed higher up the mountain to see the wall being built. Workers mixed clay and sand to make tamped earth sections. Others carefully placed heavy stone blocks and wooden supports. The wall rose higher than three grown men standing on each other's shoulders!
pages.scene: Mei observing construction workers using ancient Chinese building techniques. Some workers are tamping earth between wooden frames, others are lifting stone blocks with ropes and wooden pulleys. The partially built wall shows layers of stone, brick, and tamped earth construction.

vocabWords.definition_4_7: Parts or pieces that make up something bigger.
vocabWords.definition_7_10: Individual portions that are connected together to create one large structure or whole thing.
vocabWords.exampleSentence: The puzzle had many colorful sections that fit together to make a picture.

vocabWords.definition_4_7: Things that hold something up so it won't fall down.
vocabWords.definition_7_10: Strong pieces that bear weight and keep structures stable, like pillars holding up a bridge.
vocabWords.exampleSentence: The old bridge needed new metal supports to keep it safe for cars.

vocabWords.definition_4_7: Heavy square or rectangle pieces used for building.
vocabWords.definition_7_10: Solid chunks of stone, wood, or other materials cut into shapes for construction projects.
vocabWords.exampleSentence: The children built a tower using colorful wooden blocks in their playroom.

pages.label: Page 4
pages.text: As Mei walked along the finished sections, she discovered something amazing. Every few hundred meters stood a tall watchtower! Soldiers could light fires to send messages to the next tower, warning of danger. The smoke signals traveled faster than horses across the mountains.
pages.scene: Mei standing near a tall stone watchtower with Chinese soldiers in Qin Dynasty armor. Smoke rises from a fire on the tower's roof. The landscape shows multiple watchtowers in the distance with smoke signals between them, stretching across mountainous terrain.

vocabWords.definition_4_7: A tall building where guards look out for danger.
vocabWords.definition_7_10: A high tower where soldiers stand to watch for enemies and warn others of approaching threats.
vocabWords.exampleSentence: The lighthouse served as a watchtower for ships sailing near the rocky coast.

vocabWords.definition_4_7: Found something new or learned something you didn't know before.
vocabWords.definition_7_10: Came upon or learned about something surprising that was hidden or unknown until that moment.
vocabWords.exampleSentence: The girl discovered a secret door behind the bookshelf in her grandmother's house.

vocabWords.definition_4_7: Signs or messages that tell people important information.
vocabWords.definition_7_10: Special ways of communicating using lights, sounds, or movements to send messages across distances.
vocabWords.exampleSentence: The traffic signals changed from red to green, telling drivers they could go.

pages.label: Page 5
pages.text: From the watchtower, Mei could see the wall snaking like a stone dragon across mountains, through valleys, and over rivers. But even from this great height, the wall looked like just a thin line. 'Can people see it from the heavens?' Mei wondered aloud.
pages.scene: Mei standing on a watchtower looking out at a panoramic view of the Great Wall winding through diverse landscapes - mountains, valleys, and a river crossing. The wall appears as a thin line from this elevated perspective, emphasizing the vast scale of the terrain.

vocabWords.definition_4_7: Moving in a curvy, winding way like a snake.
vocabWords.definition_7_10: Twisting and turning in long curves, following the natural shape of the land like a serpent.
vocabWords.exampleSentence: The river was snaking through the forest, bending left and right around the trees.

vocabWords.definition_4_7: Low land between hills or mountains where water flows.
vocabWords.definition_7_10: The low, flat areas that lie between mountains or hills, often with streams running through them.
vocabWords.exampleSentence: Farmers grew corn and wheat in the fertile valleys between the rolling hills.

vocabWords.definition_4_7: The sky far above where the stars and moon are.
vocabWords.definition_7_10: The vast space high above Earth where celestial bodies like stars, planets, and the moon exist.
vocabWords.exampleSentence: At night, she loved looking up at the heavens to count the twinkling stars.

pages.label: Page 6
pages.text: A kind soldier chuckled at Mei's question. 'Little one, this wall is mighty and strong, but it's not wide enough to see from the moon and stars! That's just a story people tell. But look - you can see it clearly from any hilltop for thousands of miles.'
pages.scene: A friendly Chinese soldier in traditional Qin Dynasty armor kneeling down to speak with Mei on the watchtower. The soldier points toward the wall stretching to the horizon. Both figures are shown against the backdrop of the endless wall and mountainous landscape.

vocabWords.definition_4_7: Laughed quietly in a kind and gentle way.
vocabWords.definition_7_10: Made a soft, warm laughing sound that shows someone thinks something is funny but sweet.
vocabWords.exampleSentence: Grandpa chuckled when the puppy tried to catch its own tail in circles.

vocabWords.definition_4_7: Very strong and powerful, hard to break or move.
vocabWords.definition_7_10: Extremely strong and impressive, having great power that commands respect and admiration from others.
vocabWords.exampleSentence: The mighty oak tree survived the terrible storm that knocked down smaller trees.

vocabWords.definition_4_7: The highest point at the top of a hill.
vocabWords.definition_7_10: The peak or summit of a hill where you can see far in all directions below.
vocabWords.exampleSentence: From the hilltop, we could see our whole town spread out in the distance.

pages.label: Page 7
pages.text: Years passed, and Mei grew up watching the great wall grow too. Dynasty after dynasty added new sections and repaired old ones. The wall stretched across deserts with sand and wind, through grassy plains, and over the highest mountain peaks she had ever seen.
pages.scene: An older Mei, now a young woman, standing before different sections of the Great Wall showing various landscapes - sandy desert areas, grassy plains, and high mountain peaks. The wall shows different construction styles representing various dynasties' contributions over time.

vocabWords.definition_4_7: Fixed something that was broken or worn out.
vocabWords.definition_7_10: Restored or mended something damaged back to good condition so it works properly again.
vocabWords.exampleSentence: Mom repaired my favorite stuffed animal when the seam came loose and stuffing fell out.

vocabWords.definition_4_7: Very dry, sandy places where it almost never rains.
vocabWords.definition_7_10: Hot, dry regions with little water where only specially adapted plants and animals can survive.
vocabWords.exampleSentence: Camels can travel across deserts for days without needing to drink any water.

vocabWords.definition_4_7: Large flat areas of land covered with grass.
vocabWords.definition_7_10: Vast stretches of level ground where grass grows, perfect for animals to graze and run freely.
vocabWords.exampleSentence: Buffalo once roamed the great plains, eating grass and traveling in huge herds.

pages.label: Page 8
pages.text: When Mei became a grandmother herself, she would tell children about the amazing wall. 'It took over two thousand years to build,' she would say proudly. 'More than thirteen thousand miles long - longer than the distance around our entire earth!'
pages.scene: Elderly Mei sitting with several young children in a village setting, gesturing toward the Great Wall visible in the background. Traditional Chinese architecture surrounds them, and the children listen with wonder as she points to the ancient wall stretching across the landscape.

vocabWords.definition_4_7: With a happy feeling about something you think is special.
vocabWords.definition_7_10: Showing joy and satisfaction about an accomplishment or something important that makes you feel honored.
vocabWords.exampleSentence: She proudly showed her painting to everyone at the art show in school.

vocabWords.definition_4_7: Very long periods of time that last one hundred years each.
vocabWords.definition_7_10: Spans of one hundred years each, representing many generations of people living and dying over time.
vocabWords.exampleSentence: The ancient castle stood for many centuries before it finally crumbled into ruins.

vocabWords.definition_4_7: How far it is from one place to another place.
vocabWords.definition_7_10: The amount of space or length that separates two points, measured in miles, feet, or other units.
vocabWords.exampleSentence: The distance from my house to school is exactly three blocks down the street.

pages.label: Page 9
pages.text: Today, people from all around the world come to see the Great Wall of China. It was named a UNESCO World Heritage Site because it's so special and important to protect. Millions of visitors walk on the same stones that Mei once touched long ago.
pages.scene: Modern-day visitors of diverse ethnicities walking along a well-preserved section of the Great Wall. The ancient stone construction is clearly visible, with watchtowers in the distance. A UNESCO World Heritage Site plaque is visible, showing the wall's continued importance and preservation.

vocabWords.definition_4_7: A group that helps protect important places around the world.
vocabWords.definition_7_10: An international organization that identifies and preserves special places that are treasures for all people everywhere.
vocabWords.exampleSentence: UNESCO helps take care of amazing places like the pyramids in Egypt.

vocabWords.definition_4_7: Special things from the past that are important to remember.
vocabWords.definition_7_10: Valuable traditions, places, or objects passed down from earlier generations that represent cultural history and identity.
vocabWords.exampleSentence: The old family recipe was part of our heritage, passed down from great-grandmother.

vocabWords.definition_4_7: People who come to see a place but don't live there.
vocabWords.definition_7_10: Guests or tourists who travel to experience and explore places that are different from their homes.
vocabWords.exampleSentence: The museum had many visitors who came to see the dinosaur bones and fossils.

pages.label: Page 10
pages.text: The Great Wall stands as strong as ever, a testament to the hard work of countless people over many centuries. Like little Mei discovered long ago, it remains one of the most amazing things humans have ever built - a dragon of stone protecting China's mountains and valleys.
pages.scene: A sweeping aerial view of the Great Wall winding dramatically through mountainous terrain at sunset. The wall appears like a stone dragon across the landscape, with watchtowers dotting its length. The scene captures the wall's majesty and enduring presence across China's diverse geography.

vocabWords.definition_4_7: Proof or a sign that shows something is true.
vocabWords.definition_7_10: Living evidence that demonstrates and honors the achievements, efforts, or beliefs of people from the past.
vocabWords.exampleSentence: The beautiful garden was a testament to how much the family loved flowers.

vocabWords.definition_4_7: So many that you can't count them all.
vocabWords.definition_7_10: An enormous number that is impossible to count because there are far too many to keep track of.
vocabWords.exampleSentence: There were countless stars twinkling in the dark sky on that clear summer night.

vocabWords.definition_4_7: Keeping something safe from harm or danger.
vocabWords.definition_7_10: Guarding and defending something valuable to prevent it from being damaged, destroyed, or taken away.
vocabWords.exampleSentence: The mother bird was protecting her nest by chasing away the hungry cat.

comprehensionQuestions.question: Who had the idea to connect all the different walls into one great wall?

options.text: Little Mei's grandmother
Emperor Qin Shi Huang
The soldiers in the watchtowers

comprehensionQuestions.question: Why do you think the soldiers used smoke signals from the watchtowers?

options.text: To cook their food
To warn other soldiers about danger quickly
To make the wall look prettier

comprehensionQuestions.question: How would you feel if you were helping to build something as amazing as the Great Wall?

options.text: Proud to be part of something so special
Excited to work with so many other people
Amazed that it would last for thousands of years

predictionPause.question: What do you think Mei will learn about the Great Wall next?

options.text: Whether people can really see it from space
How long it took to build the whole wall
What happened to the wall as she grew older

h5.title: The Story of American Independence
h5.topic: American Independence from Britain. The 13 colonies were tired of unfair taxes and rules from King George III. The Boston Tea Party in 1773. Thomas Jefferson wrote the Declaration of Independence, adopted July 4, 1776. George Washington led the Continental Army.
h5.keyFacts: 13 colonies, taxation without representation, Boston Tea Party December 1773, First Continental Congress 1774, Declaration of Independence written by Thomas Jefferson adopted July 4 1776, George Washington commander of Continental Army, Liberty Bell in Philadelphia, war ended 1783 with Treaty of Paris

pages.label: Page 1
pages.text: Meet Samuel, a young boy living in Philadelphia in 1773. He lived in one of the thirteen American colonies ruled by King George III of Britain. Samuel's father was a blacksmith who worked hard every day. But the king kept making new rules and taxes that made life difficult for colonial families like Samuel's.
pages.scene: A colonial Philadelphia street scene with cobblestone roads, brick buildings with wooden shutters, and a blacksmith shop with smoke rising from the chimney. A young boy in knee-length breeches, linen shirt, and waistcoat stands with his father who wears a leather apron and rolled-up sleeves.

vocabWords.definition_4_7: Places far from home that belong to another country.
vocabWords.definition_7_10: Distant territories controlled by a more powerful country, like children living under their parents' rules.
vocabWords.exampleSentence: The ant colonies worked together to build their underground homes.

vocabWords.definition_4_7: A person who makes things out of hot metal.
vocabWords.definition_7_10: A skilled craftsman who heats metal until it glows and hammers it into useful tools and horseshoes.
vocabWords.exampleSentence: The blacksmith made a beautiful iron gate for the garden.

vocabWords.definition_4_7: Money people must pay to their government or ruler.
vocabWords.definition_7_10: Required payments that citizens must give to their government to help pay for public services.
vocabWords.exampleSentence: Dad pays taxes on our house every year to the city.

vocabWords.definition_4_7: Having to do with the time when America belonged to Britain.
vocabWords.definition_7_10: Relating to the period when American settlements were controlled by the British government across the ocean.
vocabWords.exampleSentence: The colonial house had wooden shutters and a big front porch.

pages.label: Page 2
pages.text: The colonists were angry about 'taxation without representation.' This meant King George made them pay taxes, but they had no say in making the laws. Samuel heard grown-ups talking about unfair taxes on tea, paper, and other things they needed. 'We should have a voice in making these rules!' his father said.
pages.scene: Inside a colonial home with wooden furniture, pewter dishes on shelves, and a large fireplace. Several colonial men in tricorn hats, long coats, and breeches gather around a wooden table, gesturing as they discuss. Samuel listens from nearby, wide-eyed.

vocabWords.definition_4_7: Having someone speak up for you in important meetings.
vocabWords.definition_7_10: Having elected people who can vote and speak for your interests in government decisions.
vocabWords.exampleSentence: Each classroom chose representation to speak at the school council meeting.

vocabWords.definition_4_7: People who lived in America when it belonged to Britain.
vocabWords.definition_7_10: The brave settlers who built new lives in America while still being ruled by the distant British king.
vocabWords.exampleSentence: The colonists on Mars grew vegetables in special greenhouses.

vocabWords.definition_4_7: Not right or just; treating someone badly without good reason.
vocabWords.definition_7_10: Unjust treatment that gives some people advantages while making others suffer without good cause.
vocabWords.exampleSentence: It was unfair that only some students got extra recess time.

pages.label: Page 3
pages.text: In December 1773, something exciting happened in Boston! Colonists dressed up like Native Americans and dumped British tea into the harbor. They called it the Boston Tea Party. Samuel wished he could have seen the brave colonists throwing all those tea chests into the cold, dark water!
pages.scene: Boston Harbor at night with colonial ships silhouetted against the moonlight. Men disguised as Native Americans with painted faces and feathers dump wooden tea chests over ship railings into the dark water. The scene shows the historical ships and colonial-era Boston skyline in the background.
sounds: splash of tea chests hitting water

vocabWords.definition_4_7: A safe place near the shore where boats can stay.
vocabWords.definition_7_10: A protected area of water along the coast where ships can anchor safely away from storms.
vocabWords.exampleSentence: The fishing boats returned to the harbor before the big storm arrived.

vocabWords.definition_4_7: Threw something out quickly, often making a big mess.
vocabWords.definition_7_10: Dropped or poured out carelessly in large amounts, usually to get rid of something unwanted.
vocabWords.exampleSentence: The truck dumped a pile of sand in our driveway.

vocabWords.definition_4_7: Big wooden boxes used to store and carry things.
vocabWords.definition_7_10: Large sturdy containers, usually made of wood, used for storing valuable items or transporting goods.
vocabWords.exampleSentence: Pirates buried treasure chests filled with gold coins on the island.

pages.label: Page 4
pages.text: The king was very angry about the Boston Tea Party! He made even stricter laws to punish the colonists. In 1774, representatives from the colonies met for the First Continental Congress in Philadelphia. Samuel watched important men in fancy coats walk into the building where they would make big decisions.
pages.scene: The exterior of Carpenters' Hall in Philadelphia with its brick Georgian architecture and white bell tower. Well-dressed colonial gentlemen in powdered wigs, long coats, knee breeches, and buckled shoes walk up the front steps carrying papers and leather satchels.

vocabWords.definition_4_7: More serious rules that are harder to follow or break.
vocabWords.definition_7_10: Tougher regulations with less freedom and harsher punishments for those who disobey them.
vocabWords.exampleSentence: The teacher made stricter rules about talking during quiet time.

vocabWords.definition_4_7: People chosen to speak and act for a larger group.
vocabWords.definition_7_10: Selected individuals who have the authority to make decisions and speak on behalf of many others.
vocabWords.exampleSentence: Each grade sent representatives to help plan the school's spring carnival.

vocabWords.definition_4_7: Having to do with all the American colonies working together.
vocabWords.definition_7_10: Relating to the united effort of all thirteen American colonies acting as one large group.
vocabWords.exampleSentence: The continental breakfast had foods from many different countries.

pages.label: Page 5
pages.text: By 1776, many colonists decided they wanted to be free from British rule forever. A brilliant man named Thomas Jefferson wrote a very important document called the Declaration of Independence. Samuel's father explained that it listed all the reasons why America should be its own country.
pages.scene: Thomas Jefferson, a tall redhead in colonial dress with a blue coat and white cravat, sits at a wooden desk by candlelight writing with a quill pen. Papers are scattered around him, and he looks thoughtful as he carefully writes the important document.

vocabWords.definition_4_7: Very, very smart and able to think of great ideas.
vocabWords.definition_7_10: Exceptionally intelligent and creative, capable of producing ideas that change the world for the better.
vocabWords.exampleSentence: The brilliant scientist discovered a new way to clean up ocean pollution.

vocabWords.definition_4_7: An important piece of writing that proves or explains something.
vocabWords.definition_7_10: An official written paper that contains important information, rules, or agreements that people need to follow.
vocabWords.exampleSentence: The birth certificate is an important document that proves when you were born.

vocabWords.definition_4_7: An important announcement that tells everyone what you believe or plan to do.
vocabWords.definition_7_10: A formal statement that boldly announces important decisions or beliefs to the world with great confidence.
vocabWords.exampleSentence: The student made a declaration that she would run for class president.

vocabWords.definition_4_7: Being free to make your own choices without someone else controlling you.
vocabWords.definition_7_10: The state of being completely free from control by others, able to govern yourself and make your own decisions.
vocabWords.exampleSentence: When I turned eighteen, I gained independence and moved into my own apartment.

pages.label: Page 6
pages.text: On July 4, 1776, the Continental Congress adopted Jefferson's Declaration of Independence in Philadelphia! Samuel and his family joined crowds of people celebrating in the streets. Church bells rang throughout the city. Everyone cheered because America had declared itself a free and independent nation!
pages.scene: A crowded Philadelphia street scene with colonial families celebrating. People in period dress wave their hats and cheer. Samuel and his family stand among the crowd while church bells are visible in the background. The scene shows colonial architecture with brick buildings and cobblestone streets.

vocabWords.definition_4_7: Officially accepted something and decided to use it or follow it.
vocabWords.definition_7_10: Formally approved and accepted something as official policy or law after careful consideration and voting.
vocabWords.exampleSentence: The school adopted new rules about using computers in the library.

vocabWords.definition_4_7: Announced something loudly and clearly so everyone would know.
vocabWords.definition_7_10: Made an official announcement with confidence and authority, telling everyone about an important decision or fact.
vocabWords.exampleSentence: The mayor declared that tomorrow would be a special holiday for the whole town.

vocabWords.definition_4_7: Free to make your own choices without anyone else being in charge.
vocabWords.definition_7_10: Completely free from outside control, able to govern yourself and make decisions without permission from others.
vocabWords.exampleSentence: The independent store owner could choose what products to sell without asking anyone.

pages.label: Page 7
pages.text: But King George didn't want to let America go! A war began, and the colonists needed a strong leader for their army. They chose George Washington to be the commander of the Continental Army. Samuel saw the tall, dignified man on his white horse riding through Philadelphia with his soldiers.
pages.scene: George Washington on a white horse, wearing his blue and buff Continental Army uniform with gold epaulettes and a tricorn hat. He's a tall, imposing figure riding down a Philadelphia street followed by Continental soldiers in blue uniforms carrying muskets.

vocabWords.definition_4_7: The person who leads and gives orders to all the soldiers.
vocabWords.definition_7_10: The highest-ranking military leader who makes important battle decisions and directs all the troops under their command.
vocabWords.exampleSentence: The ship's commander steered the vessel safely through the dangerous storm.

vocabWords.definition_4_7: Looking important, serious, and worthy of respect from everyone around.
vocabWords.definition_7_10: Having a calm, noble appearance and behavior that naturally makes others feel respectful and impressed.
vocabWords.exampleSentence: The dignified judge entered the courtroom wearing his black robes.

vocabWords.definition_4_7: The group of soldiers who fought for America's freedom from Britain.
vocabWords.definition_7_10: The official military force created by all thirteen colonies working together to fight the Revolutionary War.
vocabWords.exampleSentence: The Continental Army trained hard to defend their new country's independence.

pages.label: Page 8
pages.text: The war was long and difficult, lasting many years. Samuel grew taller as he waited for news of battles. Sometimes he heard the Liberty Bell ring from the State House tower when there was important news. The beautiful bronze bell had become a symbol of American freedom.
pages.scene: The Liberty Bell hanging in a bell tower with its famous crack visible. Below, young Samuel, now a bit older and taller, looks up at the bell with wonder. The scene shows the State House (Independence Hall) architecture with its red brick and white bell tower.

vocabWords.definition_4_7: Big fights between armies where soldiers use weapons to win.
vocabWords.definition_7_10: Major military conflicts where opposing forces fight with weapons to determine who will control territory or win a war.
vocabWords.exampleSentence: The chess player won many battles against stronger opponents in the tournament.

vocabWords.definition_4_7: A famous bell in Philadelphia that rang when America became free.
vocabWords.definition_7_10: The celebrated bronze bell that became a powerful symbol of American freedom and independence from British rule.
vocabWords.exampleSentence: Tourists from around the world come to see the historic Liberty Bell in its special building.

vocabWords.definition_4_7: A strong, brownish metal made by mixing copper and tin together.
vocabWords.definition_7_10: A durable reddish-brown metal created by combining copper and tin, often used for making statues and bells.
vocabWords.exampleSentence: The bronze statue of the horse gleamed in the afternoon sunlight.

pages.label: Page 9
pages.text: Finally, in 1783, wonderful news arrived! The Americans had won the war! The Treaty of Paris was signed, officially ending the fighting. King George III agreed that America was now an independent country. Samuel, now older, celebrated with his family and neighbors in the streets once again.
pages.scene: A joyous Philadelphia street celebration with colonial families dancing and embracing. Samuel, now noticeably older and taller, celebrates with his parents. People wave American flags and the scene shows the same colonial street setting but with a triumphant, festive atmosphere.

vocabWords.definition_4_7: An important agreement between countries to stop fighting or make peace.
vocabWords.definition_7_10: A formal written agreement between nations that settles disputes and establishes rules for future peaceful relationships.
vocabWords.exampleSentence: The two countries signed a treaty promising to share the river water fairly.

vocabWords.definition_4_7: In a way that makes something completely real and legally true.
vocabWords.definition_7_10: Done through proper legal channels with full authority, making something binding and recognized by everyone involved.
vocabWords.exampleSentence: The new principal was officially announced at the school board meeting last night.

vocabWords.definition_4_7: Said yes and accepted that something was right or true.
vocabWords.definition_7_10: Came to the same understanding or opinion after discussion, accepting the terms or facts presented.
vocabWords.exampleSentence: All the family members agreed that pizza would be perfect for dinner tonight.

pages.label: Page 10
pages.text: Samuel had lived through the birth of a new nation! From a young boy hearing about unfair taxes to a teenager celebrating victory, he had witnessed history. The thirteen colonies had become the United States of America. Samuel smiled, knowing he had seen his country fight for and win its precious freedom.
pages.scene: An older Samuel, now a young teenager, stands on a Philadelphia rooftop or hill overlooking the city at sunset. The colonial cityscape stretches below with church spires and the State House visible. An early American flag waves in the breeze, symbolizing the new nation's independence.

vocabWords.definition_4_7: Saw something important or special happen right in front of you.
vocabWords.definition_7_10: Personally observed and experienced significant historical events as they unfolded, becoming part of the story yourself.
vocabWords.exampleSentence: The crowd witnessed the rocket launch from the beach on that clear morning.

vocabWords.definition_4_7: Winning something important after working hard or fighting for it.
vocabWords.definition_7_10: A successful outcome achieved through struggle, effort, or competition, especially when the odds seemed difficult.
vocabWords.exampleSentence: The soccer team's victory in the championship made the whole school proud and excited.

vocabWords.definition_4_7: Something very special and valuable that you want to protect and keep safe.
vocabWords.definition_7_10: Extremely valuable and dear, worth protecting at any cost because of its importance and rarity.
vocabWords.exampleSentence: The grandmother's precious ring had been passed down through four generations of her family.

comprehensionQuestions.question: What did Samuel's father do for work in colonial Philadelphia?

options.text: He was a blacksmith who worked with metal
He was a farmer who grew vegetables
He was a sailor who worked on ships

comprehensionQuestions.question: Why do you think the colonists dressed up like Native Americans during the Boston Tea Party?

options.text: So King George wouldn't know who dumped the tea
Because it was Halloween night
Because they wanted to scare the British soldiers

comprehensionQuestions.question: How do you think Samuel felt when he heard the Liberty Bell ring with news about the war?

options.text: Excited and hopeful about what might happen
Worried and nervous about his family's safety
Curious and eager to learn the important news

predictionPause.question: What do you think will happen in the war between America and King George?

options.text: The Americans will win and become free
King George will win and keep control
They will make peace and share the land

h8.title: The Underground Railroad
h8.topic: Harriet Tubman and the Underground Railroad, a secret network of routes and safe houses used by enslaved African Americans to escape to freedom in the 1800s. Tubman escaped slavery herself in 1849, then returned south about 13 times to lead roughly 70 people to freedom. She was called Moses.
h8.keyFacts: Harriet Tubman born ~1822 in Maryland, escaped 1849, returned ~13 times, rescued ~70 people, never lost a passenger, used the North Star for navigation, safe houses called stations, conductors guided travelers, went to northern free states and Canada, Tubman also served as a Union spy during Civil War

pages.label: Page 1
pages.text: In 1822, a baby girl was born into slavery on a plantation in Maryland. Her name was Harriet. Even as a young child, Harriet was brave and strong. She worked hard in the fields under the hot sun, but she always dreamed of something more. At night, she would look up at the twinkling stars and wonder what lay beyond the plantation. Little did anyone know that this little girl would grow up to be a hero.
pages.scene: A small log cabin in rural Maryland with tobacco fields stretching into the distance. A young African American girl with short dark hair stands outside, looking up at a star-filled night sky. She wears a simple cotton dress and is barefoot. The scene shows the humble plantation setting of the 1820s.

vocabWords.definition_4_7: When people are forced to work without pay and can't leave.
vocabWords.definition_7_10: A terrible system where people are owned like property and forced to work without freedom or payment.
vocabWords.exampleSentence: Many countries around the world have laws that make slavery illegal.

vocabWords.definition_4_7: A very large farm where crops are grown by many workers.
vocabWords.definition_7_10: A huge farm in the old South where enslaved people were forced to grow crops like cotton and tobacco.
vocabWords.exampleSentence: The old plantation house is now a museum that teaches about history.

vocabWords.definition_4_7: Shining with little flashes of light that come and go.
vocabWords.definition_7_10: Sparkling with tiny lights that appear and disappear quickly, like stars dancing in the night sky.
vocabWords.exampleSentence: The twinkling lights on the Christmas tree made the whole room feel magical.

vocabWords.definition_4_7: A brave person who helps others and does good things.
vocabWords.definition_7_10: Someone who shows great courage and does important things to help other people, even when it's dangerous.
vocabWords.exampleSentence: The firefighter became a hero when she rescued the family from the burning building.

pages.label: Page 2
pages.text: As Harriet grew older, she learned that some places in the North didn't allow slavery. She heard whispered stories about something called the Underground Railroad. It wasn't a real railroad with trains and tracks. Instead, it was a secret network of brave people who helped enslaved families escape to freedom. The safe houses were called 'stations,' and the helpers were called 'conductors.' Harriet's heart filled with hope.
pages.scene: Harriet, now a young woman with determined eyes, sits in a dim cabin lit by candlelight. She leans forward listening intently to an older person sharing stories. Maps and sketches are barely visible on a wooden table. The scene captures the secretive nature of planning escapes in the 1840s.

vocabWords.definition_4_7: Talked very quietly so other people couldn't hear easily.
vocabWords.definition_7_10: Spoke in a very soft, quiet voice to keep something secret or avoid being overheard by others.
vocabWords.exampleSentence: She whispered the surprise party plans so her brother wouldn't hear.

vocabWords.definition_4_7: A group of people or things that are connected together.
vocabWords.definition_7_10: A system of people, places, or things that work together and help each other accomplish something important.
vocabWords.exampleSentence: The school has a network of computers that are all connected to share information.

vocabWords.definition_4_7: Forced to work for someone without pay or freedom.
vocabWords.definition_7_10: Made into a slave, having no freedom to leave or make choices about your own life and work.
vocabWords.exampleSentence: Many people fought to free those who were enslaved and give them their rights.

vocabWords.definition_4_7: People who helped guide others to safety on secret trips.
vocabWords.definition_7_10: Brave helpers on the Underground Railroad who led enslaved people along dangerous paths to freedom in the North.
vocabWords.exampleSentence: Train conductors help passengers find their seats and check their tickets.

pages.label: Page 3
pages.text: In 1849, when Harriet was about 27 years old, she made a brave decision. She would escape to freedom! One dark night, she began her dangerous journey north. She traveled mostly at night, hiding in forests and swamps during the day. Harriet used the North Star to guide her way, just like sailors used it to navigate the seas. After many difficult days and nights, she finally reached Pennsylvania, a free state.
pages.scene: A determined young African American woman walks through a moonlit forest, looking up at the bright North Star. She carries a small bundle and wears a long dark dress and headscarf typical of the 1840s. Tall trees create shadows around her as she navigates by starlight.

vocabWords.definition_4_7: Something that could hurt you or cause trouble.
vocabWords.definition_7_10: Full of risk and harm, where something bad could easily happen if you're not very careful.
vocabWords.exampleSentence: It's dangerous to cross the street without looking both ways first.

vocabWords.definition_4_7: Wet, muddy places with lots of plants and water.
vocabWords.definition_7_10: Wetlands filled with shallow water, thick mud, and tall grasses where it's easy to get lost or stuck.
vocabWords.exampleSentence: Alligators and frogs live in the swamps of Florida.

vocabWords.definition_4_7: To find your way from one place to another.
vocabWords.definition_7_10: To figure out which direction to go and find the right path to reach your destination safely.
vocabWords.exampleSentence: We used a map to navigate through the city and find the museum.

vocabWords.definition_4_7: A state in America where slavery was not allowed.
vocabWords.definition_7_10: A northern state where enslaved people could be free, known for important American history and the city of Philadelphia.
vocabWords.exampleSentence: Pennsylvania is famous for the Liberty Bell and where America was founded.

pages.label: Page 4
pages.text: Harriet was free! But her joy was mixed with sadness. Her family and friends were still enslaved back in Maryland. She couldn't enjoy her freedom knowing they were still suffering. 'I'm going back to help them,' she decided with fierce determination. Many people told her it was too dangerous, but Harriet had made up her mind. She would become a conductor on the Underground Railroad and lead others to freedom.
pages.scene: Harriet stands in a bustling Northern city street in Pennsylvania, with brick buildings and horse-drawn carriages. She wears a simple but clean dress and bonnet of a free woman in 1849. Her expression shows both relief and determination as she looks back toward the South.

vocabWords.definition_4_7: Being very sure you will do something no matter what.
vocabWords.definition_7_10: The strong feeling of being committed to achieving a goal, even when it's difficult or scary to keep going.
vocabWords.exampleSentence: With determination, she practiced piano every day until she could play the hard song perfectly.

vocabWords.definition_4_7: Very strong and powerful, not gentle or weak.
vocabWords.definition_7_10: Showing intense strength and boldness, like a lion protecting its cubs or a warrior in battle.
vocabWords.exampleSentence: The fierce storm brought strong winds that bent the trees almost to the ground.

vocabWords.definition_4_7: Feeling pain or sadness because bad things are happening.
vocabWords.definition_7_10: Experiencing physical pain or emotional hurt because of difficult or terrible circumstances beyond your control.
vocabWords.exampleSentence: The doctor worked hard to help reduce the patient's suffering after the accident.

pages.label: Page 5
pages.text: Harriet began her first rescue mission back to Maryland. She moved like a shadow through the night, following secret routes and staying at Underground Railroad stations. These were homes of brave people, both Black and white, who risked their lives to help. They provided food, hiding places, and directions to the next safe house. The journey was always dangerous, but Harriet was fearless and clever.
pages.scene: A modest farmhouse at night with a single candle glowing in the window - the signal for a safe station. Harriet approaches cautiously through tall grass, carrying supplies. The house shows typical 1850s architecture with wooden siding and a stone chimney. Stars shine overhead.

vocabWords.definition_4_7: To save someone from danger or a bad situation.
vocabWords.definition_7_10: To help someone escape from a dangerous or harmful place and bring them to safety where they'll be protected.
vocabWords.exampleSentence: The lifeguard had to rescue the swimmer who was caught in the strong waves.

vocabWords.definition_4_7: An important job or task that someone needs to do.
vocabWords.definition_7_10: A special assignment or goal that someone feels called to accomplish, often to help others or make a difference.
vocabWords.exampleSentence: The astronaut's mission was to repair the broken equipment on the space station.

vocabWords.definition_4_7: A dark shape made when something blocks the light.
vocabWords.definition_7_10: A dark area or shape created when an object blocks light, or moving quietly and secretly like darkness itself.
vocabWords.exampleSentence: Her shadow on the wall looked like a giant when she stood near the lamp.

vocabWords.definition_4_7: Not afraid of anything, even scary or dangerous things.
vocabWords.definition_7_10: Having the courage to face frightening situations without letting fear stop you from doing what's right or important.
vocabWords.exampleSentence: The fearless rock climber scaled the steep mountain wall without any hesitation.

pages.label: Page 6
pages.text: On her rescue missions, Harriet was like a master of disguise and tricks. Sometimes she dressed as an old woman or a man. She used clever signals like hanging clothes on a line in special ways or singing certain songs. When slave catchers got too close, she might suddenly change direction and head south, confusing them completely. Her passengers trusted her completely because she was so smart and brave.
pages.scene: Harriet in disguise wearing an old bonnet and bent over like an elderly woman, walking past suspicious-looking men on horseback. The setting shows a rural road in the 1850s with wooden fences and farmland. Her disguise is convincing and the men pay her no attention.

vocabWords.definition_4_7: Dressing or acting differently so people don't know who you are.
vocabWords.definition_7_10: Changing your appearance or behavior to hide your true identity and fool people who might be looking for you.
vocabWords.exampleSentence: For Halloween, she wore a disguise that made her look like a pirate captain.

vocabWords.definition_4_7: Special signs or actions that send secret messages to others.
vocabWords.definition_7_10: Hidden ways of communicating important information using gestures, objects, or sounds that only certain people understand the meaning of.
vocabWords.exampleSentence: The baseball coach used hand signals to tell the players what to do next.

vocabWords.definition_4_7: People who tried to find and catch runaway slaves.
vocabWords.definition_7_10: Hunters hired to track down and capture enslaved people who had escaped, bringing them back to their owners for money.
vocabWords.exampleSentence: In baseball, catchers wear special gear to protect themselves behind home plate.

vocabWords.definition_4_7: People who travel with someone else to get somewhere safe.
vocabWords.definition_7_10: On the Underground Railroad, the enslaved people who were being guided by conductors on their journey to freedom in the North.
vocabWords.exampleSentence: The airplane passengers fastened their seatbelts before takeoff.

pages.label: Page 7
pages.text: People began calling Harriet 'Moses,' after the Biblical leader who led his people out of slavery in Egypt. Just like Moses, Harriet was leading her people to the promised land of freedom. She made about 13 dangerous trips back to the South over several years. Each time, she successfully guided groups of enslaved people north to freedom in places like Pennsylvania, New York, and even Canada.
pages.scene: Harriet leads a small group of people including families with children through a moonlit landscape. Everyone carries small bundles and moves quietly in single file. Harriet, wearing her characteristic headscarf, points toward the North Star. The scene shows the courage of families risking everything for freedom.

vocabWords.definition_4_7: Having to do with stories from the Bible.
vocabWords.definition_7_10: Related to the holy book called the Bible, which contains important religious stories and teachings for many people.
vocabWords.exampleSentence: The Biblical story of Noah and the ark is known around the world.

vocabWords.definition_4_7: A country in Africa famous for pyramids and ancient history.
vocabWords.definition_7_10: An ancient land in Africa where, according to the Bible, Moses led enslaved people to freedom long ago.
vocabWords.exampleSentence: Egypt is famous for its pyramids, mummies, and the mighty Nile River.

vocabWords.definition_4_7: A special place that was said to be wonderful and free.
vocabWords.definition_7_10: A land that was pledged or guaranteed to be a place of hope, freedom, and better life for those seeking escape from suffering.
vocabWords.exampleSentence: She promised to call her grandmother every Sunday without fail.

vocabWords.definition_4_7: Doing something the right way and getting good results.
vocabWords.definition_7_10: Accomplishing a goal or completing a task in the way you planned, achieving the outcome you hoped for without failure.
vocabWords.exampleSentence: She successfully learned to ride her bike after practicing for weeks in the park.

pages.label: Page 8
pages.text: During her years as a conductor, Harriet rescued about 70 people from slavery. This included her own family members, friends, and strangers who needed help. The most amazing thing was that she never lost a single passenger! Not one person was ever captured while traveling with her. Her perfect record made her famous among both those seeking freedom and those trying to stop her.
pages.scene: A joyful reunion scene in a Northern safe house where Harriet embraces family members she has successfully rescued. The room shows simple but comfortable 1850s furnishings with a warm fireplace. Multiple generations are present, showing how she rescued entire families.

vocabWords.definition_4_7: Saved people from danger and brought them to safety.
vocabWords.definition_7_10: Successfully helped people escape from harmful situations and guided them to places where they would be protected and free.
vocabWords.exampleSentence: The firefighters rescued three cats from the tree during the storm.

vocabWords.definition_4_7: Caught and taken prisoner by someone trying to hurt you.
vocabWords.definition_7_10: Seized and held against your will by people who want to bring you back to a place where you'll be harmed or enslaved again.
vocabWords.exampleSentence: The butterfly was captured in a net but then gently released back into the garden.

vocabWords.definition_4_7: A person traveling with a guide to reach freedom.
vocabWords.definition_7_10: Someone being helped to escape slavery by traveling secretly with a conductor on the Underground Railroad toward the North.
vocabWords.exampleSentence: Every passenger on the bus had to show their ticket to the driver.

vocabWords.definition_4_7: A list that shows how well someone did at something.
vocabWords.definition_7_10: An official account of achievements or results that shows how successful someone has been at completing important tasks over time.
vocabWords.exampleSentence: The swimmer broke the school record by finishing the race faster than anyone before.

pages.label: Page 9
pages.text: When the Civil War began in 1861, Harriet found new ways to fight against slavery. She worked as a spy for the Union Army, using her knowledge of secret routes and her network of contacts in the South. She helped plan raids and gather important information. In 1863, she even helped lead a raid in South Carolina that freed more than 700 enslaved people in a single day!
pages.scene: Harriet in simple dark clothing stands near a river with Union soldiers, pointing to locations on a hand-drawn map. Military boats are visible in the background. The scene depicts her role as a spy and guide during the Civil War in the 1860s.

vocabWords.definition_4_7: A war fought between people from the same country.
vocabWords.definition_7_10: The American war from 1861-1865 when northern and southern states fought over slavery and keeping the country together.
vocabWords.exampleSentence: Many families were divided during the Civil War because they lived in different states.

vocabWords.definition_4_7: Someone who secretly finds out information about enemies.
vocabWords.definition_7_10: A person who gathers secret information about the opposing side during war or conflict, often by disguising their true identity.
vocabWords.exampleSentence: The spy used a special code to send secret messages to headquarters.

vocabWords.definition_4_7: The northern states that wanted to end slavery.
vocabWords.definition_7_10: The United States government and northern states that fought against the South to keep the country together and end slavery.
vocabWords.exampleSentence: The Union army wore blue uniforms during the Civil War battles.

vocabWords.definition_4_7: Quick surprise attacks to rescue people or get things.
