vocabWords.definition_7_10: Sudden military attacks designed to quickly accomplish a specific goal, like freeing prisoners or destroying enemy supplies, then escaping fast.
vocabWords.exampleSentence: The police conducted raids on several buildings to arrest the criminals hiding inside.

pages.label: Page 10
pages.text: Harriet Tubman became one of the most famous heroes in American history. Her courage, intelligence, and determination helped dozens of people escape slavery and find freedom. The Underground Railroad that she helped operate saved thousands of lives. Today, we remember Harriet as a symbol of bravery and hope. Her story shows us that one person can make a tremendous difference in the world by standing up for what's right.
pages.scene: An older Harriet Tubman sitting peacefully in a garden, surrounded by children of different backgrounds listening to her stories. She wears a white headscarf and simple dress of the late 1800s. The setting shows her later years when she was recognized as a national hero.

vocabWords.definition_4_7: Being brave enough to do scary things that help others.
vocabWords.definition_7_10: The strength to face danger, pain, or difficulty without giving up, especially when fighting for what you believe is right.
vocabWords.exampleSentence: It took courage for the little boy to speak up when he saw someone being bullied.

vocabWords.definition_4_7: Being very smart and able to figure out hard problems.
vocabWords.definition_7_10: The ability to learn quickly, understand complex ideas, and use clever thinking to solve difficult problems and make good decisions.
vocabWords.exampleSentence: Her intelligence helped her become the youngest person ever to win the science competition.

vocabWords.definition_4_7: Something that stands for an important idea or feeling.
vocabWords.definition_7_10: A person, object, or image that represents bigger ideas like freedom, hope, or courage that inspire people everywhere.
vocabWords.exampleSentence: The American flag is a symbol of freedom and democracy around the world.

vocabWords.definition_4_7: Really, really big and important in a good way.
vocabWords.definition_7_10: Extremely large, powerful, or significant in a way that amazes people and makes a lasting impact on many lives.
vocabWords.exampleSentence: The tremendous thunderstorm brought lightning, hail, and winds that shook the whole house.

comprehensionQuestions.question: What did people call the secret network that helped enslaved people escape to freedom?

options.text: The Underground Railroad
The Freedom Train
The Secret Highway

comprehensionQuestions.question: Why do you think Harriet was called 'Moses'?

options.text: Because like Moses in the Bible, she led people out of slavery
Because Moses was her favorite name to use
Because she looked like the Biblical Moses

comprehensionQuestions.question: How would you feel if you were as brave as Harriet Tubman?

options.text: Proud that I could help so many people
Excited to make a difference in the world
Happy knowing I stood up for what's right

predictionPause.question: What do you think will happen when Harriet starts trying to rescue other people?

options.text: She'll successfully help many people escape to freedom
She'll get caught by the slave catchers
She'll decide it's too dangerous and stop

h9.title: Pompeii: The City Frozen in Time
h9.topic: The eruption of Mount Vesuvius in 79 AD that buried the Roman city of Pompeii under volcanic ash. The city was a thriving Roman town of about 11,000 people. The eruption happened on August 24, preserving the city almost perfectly under ash. It was rediscovered in 1748.
h9.keyFacts: Pompeii near modern Naples Italy, population ~11000, eruption of Vesuvius August 24 79 AD, city buried under 13-20 feet of volcanic ash and pumice, also buried nearby Herculaneum, rediscovered 1748, preserved buildings frescoes mosaics and even food, plaster casts of victims, now a UNESCO World Heritage Site and major archaeological site

pages.label: Page 1
pages.text: In the year 79 AD, near what we now call Naples, Italy, lived a Roman city called Pompeii. About 11,000 people called this bustling place home. Young Marcus loved living here, where colorful buildings lined stone streets and the sweet smell of fresh bread drifted from bakeries. Behind the city loomed Mount Vesuvius, a tall mountain that had been quiet for hundreds of years. Everyone thought it was just a peaceful hill covered in grapevines and olive trees.
pages.scene: A vibrant Roman city street in Pompeii with young Marcus, a boy wearing a short tunic and sandals, walking past red and yellow painted buildings with terracotta roofs. Mount Vesuvius rises peacefully in the background, covered in green vegetation. Roman citizens in togas and tunics go about their daily business.

vocabWords.definition_4_7: Very busy with lots of people moving around quickly.
vocabWords.definition_7_10: Full of activity and energy, like a busy marketplace where everyone is rushing around doing things.
vocabWords.exampleSentence: The bustling school hallway was filled with students hurrying to their next class.

vocabWords.definition_4_7: Stood very tall and big, looking scary or important.
vocabWords.definition_7_10: Rose up high and impressive in the distance, creating a powerful presence that catches your attention.
vocabWords.exampleSentence: The dark storm clouds loomed over the beach, making everyone run for shelter.

vocabWords.definition_4_7: Moved slowly and gently through the air like a soft breeze.
vocabWords.definition_7_10: Floated smoothly and quietly from one place to another, like leaves floating on water.
vocabWords.exampleSentence: The music drifted from the neighbor's house into our backyard during the party.

vocabWords.definition_4_7: Plants that grow grapes for eating or making juice and wine.
vocabWords.definition_7_10: Climbing plants with twisted stems that produce bunches of sweet grapes hanging like nature's candy.
vocabWords.exampleSentence: The farmer carefully tended his grapevines to make sure they would grow juicy grapes.

pages.label: Page 2
pages.text: Marcus spent his days exploring Pompeii's amazing sights. He loved watching gladiator fights in the great amphitheater, where 20,000 people could cheer at once. In the forum, the city's main square, merchants sold everything from fish to fine jewelry. Beautiful houses had walls painted with bright frescoes showing gods, animals, and garden scenes. Some homes even had running water and heated floors! Marcus thought Pompeii was the most wonderful city in the Roman Empire.
pages.scene: The Roman forum of Pompeii with Marcus standing among marble columns and statues. Merchants display goods under colorful awnings while Roman citizens in togas conduct business. In the background, the amphitheater is visible with its arched stone structure.

vocabWords.definition_4_7: A big round building where people sit to watch shows.
vocabWords.definition_7_10: A large outdoor theater built in a circle with rows of seats rising up so everyone can see the stage.
vocabWords.exampleSentence: The school play was performed in the outdoor amphitheater behind the gymnasium.

vocabWords.definition_4_7: People who buy and sell things to make money.
vocabWords.definition_7_10: Business people who travel around selling goods like spices, jewelry, and other valuable items to customers.
vocabWords.exampleSentence: The merchants at the farmer's market sold fresh vegetables and homemade jam.

vocabWords.definition_4_7: Beautiful paintings made right on wet wall plaster.
vocabWords.definition_7_10: Colorful artwork painted directly onto wet plaster walls that becomes permanent when it dries completely.
vocabWords.exampleSentence: The artist painted frescoes on the library ceiling showing scenes from famous books.

vocabWords.definition_4_7: The main meeting place in the center of town.
vocabWords.definition_7_10: The most important public square in a Roman city where people gathered to shop, talk, and conduct business.
vocabWords.exampleSentence: Students gathered in the school forum to discuss plans for the spring carnival.

pages.label: Page 3
pages.text: On the morning of August 24th, 79 AD, Marcus woke up like any other day. But something felt different. The ground had been shaking slightly for several days, making dishes rattle and dogs bark nervously. Many people in Pompeii weren't too worried – small earthquakes happened sometimes. Marcus's father said they would go to the market as planned. Nobody knew that Mount Vesuvius was about to wake up from its long sleep.
pages.scene: Marcus in his family's Roman house atrium with a central pool, looking concerned as pottery rattles on shelves. His father, wearing a white toga, gestures calmly while sunlight streams through the open roof. The house has red and black painted walls with geometric patterns.

vocabWords.definition_4_7: Moving back and forth quickly in a trembling way.
vocabWords.definition_7_10: Vibrating or trembling with quick movements, like when you're very cold or scared and can't hold still.
vocabWords.exampleSentence: The washing machine was shaking so much that it moved across the laundry room floor.

vocabWords.definition_4_7: Make quick knocking sounds by hitting against something.
vocabWords.definition_7_10: Create a series of sharp clicking or clattering noises when objects bump together repeatedly and rapidly.
vocabWords.exampleSentence: The coins in my pocket rattle when I walk up the stairs.

vocabWords.definition_4_7: When the ground shakes and moves because of underground movements.
vocabWords.definition_7_10: Natural disasters where the earth's surface trembles and shifts due to powerful forces moving deep underground.
vocabWords.exampleSentence: The earthquake made all the books fall off the library shelves onto the floor.

pages.label: Page 4
pages.text: Around midday, a tremendous BOOM echoed across the land. Marcus and everyone in Pompeii looked up to see Mount Vesuvius exploding with a massive column of ash and rock shooting high into the sky. The column looked like a giant pine tree made of gray and black smoke. The sun disappeared behind the ash cloud, and the day turned as dark as night. Hot pumice stones, light gray rocks full of air bubbles, began raining down on the city.
pages.scene: Mount Vesuvius erupting with a towering column of dark ash rising into the sky like a pine tree. Marcus and other Romans in the street point up in terror, their togas and tunics whipping in the wind. Gray pumice stones fall like hail around them as the sky darkens ominously.

vocabWords.definition_4_7: Extremely big, loud, or powerful in an amazing way.
vocabWords.definition_7_10: Incredibly huge or intense, so impressive that it makes you feel amazed and maybe a little overwhelmed.
vocabWords.exampleSentence: The tremendous thunderclap made everyone in the theater jump out of their seats.

vocabWords.definition_4_7: A tall shape that goes straight up like a pole.
vocabWords.definition_7_10: A vertical structure that rises high into the air, like a pillar supporting a building or smoke rising upward.
vocabWords.exampleSentence: A column of ants marched in a straight line toward the picnic blanket.

vocabWords.definition_4_7: Light gray rocks full of tiny air holes from volcanoes.
vocabWords.definition_7_10: Special volcanic rocks that are surprisingly light because they're filled with air bubbles from hot lava cooling quickly.
vocabWords.exampleSentence: The pumice stone felt rough and scratchy when I used it to smooth my feet.

vocabWords.definition_4_7: Bursting apart with great force and loud noise.
vocabWords.definition_7_10: Breaking apart violently and suddenly with tremendous power, sending pieces flying in all directions with loud sounds.
vocabWords.exampleSentence: The fireworks were exploding in the sky with bright colors and loud booms.

pages.label: Page 5
pages.text: The pumice stones fell faster and thicker. Marcus's family, like many others, made a difficult choice. Some people decided to flee the city immediately, loading their belongings onto carts and heading away from the mountain. Others, including Marcus's family, decided to stay inside their sturdy stone house and wait for the eruption to stop. They covered their faces with cloth to keep from breathing the ash and huddled together as the stones piled up outside their door.
pages.scene: Inside Marcus's Roman house, his family huddles together with cloths over their faces. Through the doorway, pumice stones are visible piling up in the street. Some neighbors hurry past with loaded carts, while ash swirls in the darkened air.

vocabWords.definition_4_7: Run away quickly to escape from something dangerous.
vocabWords.definition_7_10: Leave a place as fast as possible to get away from danger or something that scares you.
vocabWords.exampleSentence: The rabbits flee into the bushes when they hear the dog barking.

vocabWords.definition_4_7: Things that you own and are important to you.
vocabWords.definition_7_10: Personal possessions and items that belong to you, like clothes, toys, and other special things you keep.
vocabWords.exampleSentence: I packed all my belongings into boxes when we moved to our new house.

vocabWords.definition_4_7: Very strong and not easy to break or knock down.
vocabWords.definition_7_10: Built solidly and tough enough to last a long time without breaking, even in difficult conditions.
vocabWords.exampleSentence: The sturdy wooden table didn't even wobble when I stood on it to reach the ceiling.

vocabWords.definition_4_7: Crowded close together to stay warm and feel safe.
vocabWords.definition_7_10: Gathered in a tight group with everyone pressed close together, usually for warmth, comfort, or protection.
vocabWords.exampleSentence: The puppies huddled together in their bed to keep warm during the cold night.

pages.label: Page 6
pages.text: For hours, the pumice continued to fall. The gray stones piled up 6 feet deep, then 10 feet, then even higher. Roofs began to collapse under the weight. The air became thick and hard to breathe. Many people tried to escape, but it was becoming very dangerous. Marcus and his family, along with thousands of other people in Pompeii, could not survive the poisonous gases and ash that filled the air. The great city was being buried alive under the volcanic debris.
pages.scene: Pompeii's streets buried deep in gray pumice stones with only the tops of buildings visible. The air is thick with swirling ash and the sky remains dark. Some collapsed roofs show the weight of the accumulation, creating a haunting, desolate scene.

vocabWords.definition_4_7: Fall down suddenly because they can't hold the weight anymore.
vocabWords.definition_7_10: Cave in or crumble completely when something becomes too heavy or weak to stay standing up.
vocabWords.exampleSentence: The sandcastle will collapse when the big waves reach it on the beach.

vocabWords.definition_4_7: Dangerous chemicals that can hurt or kill living things.
vocabWords.definition_7_10: Containing harmful substances that can make people very sick or cause death when breathed in or swallowed.
vocabWords.exampleSentence: Some mushrooms in the forest are poisonous and should never be eaten by people.

vocabWords.definition_4_7: Broken pieces of things scattered around after something gets destroyed.
vocabWords.definition_7_10: Scattered fragments and wreckage left behind when buildings, rocks, or other objects break apart and fall.
vocabWords.exampleSentence: After the storm, debris from broken tree branches covered the playground and parking lot.

pages.label: Page 7
pages.text: Mount Vesuvius continued erupting for two days. When it finally stopped, Pompeii lay buried under 13 to 20 feet of ash and pumice. The nearby city of Herculaneum was also buried by the same eruption. Both cities vanished completely, as if they had never existed. Grass and trees eventually grew over the ash, and for many centuries, people forgot exactly where the great city of Pompeii had been. The mountain had hidden one of history's greatest treasures.
pages.scene: A peaceful landscape with grassy hills and scattered trees growing over the buried city. Mount Vesuvius stands quiet in the background. The scene shows the passage of time, with no visible signs of the city beneath, just gentle rolling terrain where Pompeii once stood.

vocabWords.definition_4_7: Shooting out hot lava, ash, and rocks from inside.
vocabWords.definition_7_10: Exploding violently and sending molten rock, ash, and gas high into the air from deep underground.
vocabWords.exampleSentence: The science project volcano started erupting when we mixed the baking soda and vinegar.

vocabWords.definition_4_7: Disappeared completely so no one could see them anymore.
vocabWords.definition_7_10: Disappeared so completely that it seemed like they had never existed at all, leaving no trace behind.
vocabWords.exampleSentence: My homework vanished from my backpack and I couldn't find it anywhere in the house.

vocabWords.definition_4_7: Very valuable and special things that people want to keep safe.
vocabWords.definition_7_10: Precious objects or discoveries that are extremely valuable and important, like gold, jewels, or rare artifacts.
vocabWords.exampleSentence: The pirates buried their treasures on a secret island marked with an X.

vocabWords.definition_4_7: Very long periods of time lasting hundreds of years.
vocabWords.definition_7_10: Extremely long stretches of time, each one lasting one hundred years, spanning many generations of people.
vocabWords.exampleSentence: The ancient oak tree has been growing in our town for several centuries.

pages.label: Page 8
pages.text: In 1748, almost 1,700 years later, something amazing happened. Workers digging a well discovered pieces of ancient buildings buried underground. Archaeologists – scientists who study old things – realized they had found the lost city of Pompeii! They began carefully digging and brushing away the ash and pumice. What they found was incredible: a Roman city preserved almost perfectly, like a snapshot frozen in time from that terrible day in 79 AD.
pages.scene: 18th-century archaeologists in period clothing using brushes and tools to carefully uncover ancient Roman buildings. Partially excavated walls with red frescoes emerge from the ash, while workers with wheelbarrows remove debris. The scene shows the excitement of discovery.

vocabWords.definition_4_7: Scientists who dig up and study very old things.
vocabWords.definition_7_10: Special scientists who carefully uncover and examine ancient objects, buildings, and artifacts to learn about past civilizations.
vocabWords.exampleSentence: The archaeologists discovered dinosaur bones buried deep in the desert sand dunes.

vocabWords.definition_4_7: Kept safe and protected so they don't get ruined.
vocabWords.definition_7_10: Protected and maintained in excellent condition for a very long time, preventing decay or damage from occurring.
vocabWords.exampleSentence: The flowers were preserved in the book pages, keeping their colors bright for many years.

vocabWords.definition_4_7: So amazing that it's hard to believe it's real.
vocabWords.definition_7_10: Absolutely extraordinary and wonderful, so impressive that it almost seems impossible or too good to be true.
vocabWords.exampleSentence: The circus performer did incredible tricks on the tightrope that made everyone gasp with amazement.

vocabWords.definition_4_7: A picture that shows exactly what happened at one moment.
vocabWords.definition_7_10: A perfect capture of a single moment in time, preserving exactly how things looked and felt at that instant.
vocabWords.exampleSentence: The family photo was a snapshot of our happy vacation day at the amusement park.

pages.label: Page 9
pages.text: The archaeologists discovered amazing things in Pompeii. Beautiful wall paintings called frescoes still showed bright reds, yellows, and blues. Mosaic floors made of tiny colored tiles displayed pictures of fish, animals, and people. They found loaves of bread still in bakery ovens, eggs, and even jars of fish sauce! The ash had protected everything like a giant blanket, preserving buildings, artwork, and even the food that people were eating on that August day long ago.
pages.scene: Excavated Roman buildings showing colorful frescoes on walls, intricate mosaic floors, and archaeologists carefully examining preserved artifacts. Ancient bread loaves sit in stone ovens, and decorated pottery lines shelves, all remarkably preserved.

vocabWords.definition_4_7: Art made by putting together lots of tiny colored pieces.
vocabWords.definition_7_10: Beautiful artwork created by carefully arranging thousands of small colored tiles or stones to form pictures and patterns.
vocabWords.exampleSentence: The swimming pool had a mosaic of a dolphin made from blue and white tiles.

vocabWords.definition_4_7: Showed something so people could see and enjoy it.
vocabWords.definition_7_10: Presented or exhibited something in a way that makes it easy for people to view and appreciate.
vocabWords.exampleSentence: The art teacher displayed our best drawings on the bulletin board in the hallway.

vocabWords.definition_4_7: Kept safe from things that could hurt or damage them.
vocabWords.definition_7_10: Shielded and guarded from harm, damage, or destruction by creating a safe barrier or covering around them.
vocabWords.exampleSentence: The baby birds were protected by their mother, who watched over them in the nest.

pages.label: Page 10
pages.text: Today, Pompeii is a UNESCO World Heritage Site, which means it's protected as one of the world's most important archaeological treasures. Visitors from around the globe come to walk the same stone streets where Marcus once played. They can see the forum, the amphitheater, and the houses with their beautiful frescoes. Pompeii teaches us how Romans lived almost 2,000 years ago. Though the eruption was a tragedy, it accidentally gave us an amazing window into the past that helps us understand ancient history.
pages.scene: Modern-day tourists walking through the excavated streets of Pompeii, with Mount Vesuvius visible in the background. Ancient Roman buildings, columns, and frescoes are clearly visible, while a tour guide points out features to interested visitors of all ages.

vocabWords.definition_4_7: A world organization that protects important historical places.
vocabWords.definition_7_10: An international organization that identifies and protects the most valuable cultural and historical sites around the world.
vocabWords.exampleSentence: UNESCO helps protect ancient pyramids and other important places for future generations to visit.

vocabWords.definition_4_7: Having to do with digging up and studying old things.
vocabWords.definition_7_10: Related to the scientific study of ancient civilizations through carefully examining their buried artifacts, buildings, and remains.
vocabWords.exampleSentence: The archaeological dig at the school playground uncovered old pottery from Native Americans.

vocabWords.definition_4_7: Something very sad and terrible that happens to people.
vocabWords.definition_7_10: A devastating and heartbreaking event that causes great suffering and loss, making people feel deep sadness.
vocabWords.exampleSentence: The forest fire was a tragedy that destroyed many animals' homes and made everyone very upset.

vocabWords.definition_4_7: Very, very old from a long time ago.
vocabWords.definition_7_10: Extremely old, dating back thousands of years to times when life was completely different from today.
vocabWords.exampleSentence: The ancient castle had thick stone walls and towers that were built over 800 years ago.

comprehensionQuestions.question: What did Mount Vesuvius shoot high into the sky when it erupted?

options.text: A massive column of ash and rock
Bright colorful fireworks
Hot water and steam

comprehensionQuestions.question: Why do you think archaeologists were so excited when they found Pompeii?

options.text: They found lots of gold and treasure to sell
It was perfectly preserved like a time capsule
They wanted to rebuild the city for tourists

comprehensionQuestions.question: How would you feel if you lived in Pompeii and saw Mount Vesuvius start erupting?

options.text: Scared and worried about my family's safety
Amazed by the incredible power of nature
Confused and unsure what to do next

predictionPause.question: What do you think will happen to Marcus and his family as the pumice stones keep falling?

options.text: They will escape safely to another city
They will be trapped and buried by the ash
The eruption will stop and they'll be okay

h10.title: The Wright Brothers at Kitty Hawk
h10.topic: Wilbur and Orville Wright, two brothers from Dayton, Ohio who ran a bicycle shop and built the first successful powered airplane. On December 17, 1903, at Kitty Hawk, North Carolina, they made four flights. The first lasted 12 seconds and covered 120 feet. The longest was 59 seconds.
h10.keyFacts: Wilbur born 1867 Orville born 1871, owned a bicycle shop in Dayton Ohio, studied birds for wing design, built wind tunnel for testing, the Wright Flyer was their plane, first flight December 17 1903, Kitty Hawk North Carolina chosen for steady winds and soft sand, first flight 12 seconds 120 feet by Orville, four flights that day longest 59 seconds 852 feet by Wilbur

pages.label: Page 1
pages.text: In Dayton, Ohio, two brothers worked together in their bicycle shop. Wilbur Wright was born in 1867, and his younger brother Orville was born in 1871. They loved fixing bicycles and building new things with their hands. But the Wright brothers had a big dream that reached far beyond bicycles. They dreamed of flying through the sky like the birds they watched outside their shop window every day.
pages.scene: The Wright Brothers' bicycle shop in Dayton, Ohio in the early 1900s. Two men in white shirts, dark vests, and bow ties work among hanging bicycles. Wilbur is taller and clean-shaven, Orville shorter with a mustache. The shop has wooden floors, large windows, and various bicycle parts and tools scattered around workbenches.

vocabWords.definition_4_7: A two-wheeled vehicle that you pedal with your feet.
vocabWords.definition_7_10: A two-wheeled machine you ride by pushing pedals with your feet to make it move forward.
vocabWords.exampleSentence: She rode her bicycle to school every morning.

vocabWords.definition_4_7: Farther away than something or more than something.
vocabWords.definition_7_10: Going past a certain point or limit, reaching farther than what you can see or touch.
vocabWords.exampleSentence: The mountain trail continued beyond the forest.

vocabWords.definition_4_7: To hope for something special to happen someday.
vocabWords.definition_7_10: To imagine and wish for something wonderful that you hope will come true in the future.
vocabWords.exampleSentence: Maria dreamed of becoming a famous artist when she grew up.

pages.label: Page 2
pages.text: The brothers spent hours watching birds soar and glide through the air. They noticed how birds spread their wings wide to catch the wind, and how they tilted their wings to turn and dive. Wilbur and Orville took careful notes about everything they saw. They studied the curved shape of bird wings and wondered if they could build wings that worked the same way for a flying machine.
pages.scene: The Wright brothers standing in a field outside Dayton, looking up at birds flying overhead. Wilbur holds a notebook and pencil, sketching bird wing shapes. Several birds are shown in flight above them with wings spread wide. The sky is blue with white clouds, and there are trees and grass around them.

vocabWords.definition_4_7: To fly high up in the sky smoothly.
vocabWords.definition_7_10: To glide gracefully through the air at great heights, like an eagle riding the wind.
vocabWords.exampleSentence: The kite began to soar above the tall trees.

vocabWords.definition_4_7: To move smoothly through the air without flapping.
vocabWords.definition_7_10: To float or slide effortlessly through the air, moving gracefully without effort or struggle.
vocabWords.exampleSentence: The paper airplane would glide across the classroom.

vocabWords.definition_4_7: Bent in a smooth, rounded shape like a smile.
vocabWords.definition_7_10: Having a smooth, bending line that is not straight, like the arc of a rainbow.
vocabWords.exampleSentence: The curved path wound around the garden pond.

pages.label: Page 3
pages.text: Back in their shop, the brothers built something very special - a wind tunnel! It was a wooden box with a fan that blew air over tiny model wings. They tested hundreds of different wing shapes to see which ones worked best in the moving air. This wind tunnel helped them learn secrets about flying that no one had ever discovered before. It was like having their own scientific laboratory right in the bicycle shop.
pages.scene: Inside the bicycle shop, showing a rectangular wooden wind tunnel with a fan at one end. The Wright brothers peer into the tunnel, testing small model wing shapes. There are papers with sketches and measurements spread across a workbench. Oil lamps provide warm light in the workshop setting.

vocabWords.definition_4_7: A long hollow space that goes through something.
vocabWords.definition_7_10: A long, enclosed passageway that allows things to move through from one end to the other.
vocabWords.exampleSentence: The train traveled through the mountain tunnel.

vocabWords.definition_4_7: A very large number of things, many many things.
vocabWords.definition_7_10: A great many of something, much more than you could easily count on your fingers.
vocabWords.exampleSentence: There were hundreds of stars twinkling in the night sky.

vocabWords.definition_4_7: A special room where people do science experiments.
vocabWords.definition_7_10: A place equipped with tools and equipment where scientists conduct experiments and make discoveries.
vocabWords.exampleSentence: The scientist mixed colorful chemicals in the laboratory.

pages.label: Page 4
pages.text: After years of studying and testing, the brothers were ready to build their flying machine. They called it the Wright Flyer. It had two long wings made of white cloth stretched over a wooden frame. The plane had a small engine they built themselves, and two wooden propellers to push it through the air. The Wright Flyer looked delicate, but it was carefully designed using everything they had learned about flight.
pages.scene: The Wright Flyer being assembled in a large workshop. The biplane has white fabric wings stretched over wooden frames, with wooden propellers visible. Both brothers work on different parts of the aircraft. Tools, wood shavings, and fabric are scattered around. The plane appears lightweight but sturdy.

vocabWords.definition_4_7: A machine that makes power to move things.
vocabWords.definition_7_10: A mechanical device that creates power by burning fuel, making vehicles and machines move.
vocabWords.exampleSentence: The car's engine made a loud rumbling sound.

vocabWords.definition_4_7: Spinning blades that push air or water backward.
vocabWords.definition_7_10: Rotating blades that spin rapidly to push against air or water, creating forward motion.
vocabWords.exampleSentence: The boat's propellers churned the water as it sped away.

vocabWords.definition_4_7: Something that looks fragile and could break easily.
vocabWords.definition_7_10: Appearing fragile and needing careful handling, like something beautiful but easily damaged.
vocabWords.exampleSentence: The delicate flower petals felt soft and smooth.

pages.label: Page 5
pages.text: The brothers needed to find the perfect place to test their flying machine. They chose Kitty Hawk, North Carolina, because it had steady winds that would help lift their plane into the air. The soft sand dunes would make a safer landing than hard ground. In December 1903, Wilbur and Orville packed up their Wright Flyer and traveled by train to the windy coast of North Carolina.
pages.scene: A train station scene with the Wright brothers loading crates containing their disassembled airplane parts onto a train car. They wear dark coats and bowler hats typical of 1903. The locomotive is a period steam engine with black smoke. Other passengers in early 1900s clothing wait on the platform.

vocabWords.definition_4_7: Something that doesn't change and stays the same.
vocabWords.definition_7_10: Remaining constant and reliable, not changing or moving around in an unpredictable way.
vocabWords.exampleSentence: She held the flashlight with a steady hand.

vocabWords.definition_4_7: Big hills made of sand that wind has piled up.
vocabWords.definition_7_10: Large mounds of sand that have been shaped and moved by wind over time.
vocabWords.exampleSentence: The children loved sliding down the tall sand dunes.

vocabWords.definition_4_7: To go from one place to another place.
vocabWords.definition_7_10: To journey from one location to another, often covering a long distance by vehicle.
vocabWords.exampleSentence: Our family traveled to the beach for vacation.

pages.label: Page 6
pages.text: At Kitty Hawk, the brothers set up camp near the sandy dunes. The December wind was cold and strong - perfect for flying, but chilly for the two men from Ohio! They rebuilt their Wright Flyer and tested it carefully. On the morning of December 17, 1903, the wind was blowing just right. Today would be the day they would try to make history.
pages.scene: The Wright brothers' camp at Kitty Hawk with a simple wooden building and tents on sandy dunes. The assembled Wright Flyer sits nearby. Ocean and dunes stretch into the distance. Both men wear heavy coats and caps against the cold December wind. Seagrass bends in the wind.

vocabWords.definition_4_7: A place where people stay in tents outdoors.
vocabWords.definition_7_10: A temporary outdoor living area where people sleep in tents and cook over fires.
vocabWords.exampleSentence: We roasted marshmallows around the camp fire.

vocabWords.definition_4_7: To build something again after it was taken apart.
vocabWords.definition_7_10: To construct something once more, usually after it was damaged or needed to be improved.
vocabWords.exampleSentence: After the storm, they rebuilt their tree house even stronger.

vocabWords.definition_4_7: Important things that happened in the past long ago.
vocabWords.definition_7_10: The record of important events and achievements that happened in earlier times and shaped our world.
vocabWords.exampleSentence: We learned about dinosaurs in our history lesson.

pages.label: Page 7
pages.text: The brothers flipped a coin to see who would pilot the first flight, and Orville won! He lay down on the lower wing of the Wright Flyer while Wilbur ran alongside, holding the wing to help balance the plane. Five local men came to watch this amazing attempt. The engine sputtered to life, the propellers began to spin, and the Wright Flyer started rolling forward across the sand.
pages.scene: Orville lying prone on the lower wing of the Wright Flyer at the moment of takeoff. Wilbur runs alongside holding the wing. Five men in dark coats and hats stand watching in the background. The biplane's propellers are spinning, and sand is being kicked up. The scene captures the exact moment of the first flight attempt.

vocabWords.definition_4_7: To toss a coin up in the air quickly.
vocabWords.definition_7_10: To throw a coin spinning through the air to make a random choice between two options.
vocabWords.exampleSentence: They flipped a coin to decide who would go first.

vocabWords.definition_4_7: The person who flies and controls an airplane.
vocabWords.definition_7_10: Someone trained to operate and navigate aircraft, responsible for safely controlling the flight.
vocabWords.exampleSentence: The pilot announced that we would land in ten minutes.

vocabWords.definition_4_7: To make quick popping sounds like a broken machine.
vocabWords.definition_7_10: To make irregular bursts of sound, like an engine that's struggling to start or run smoothly.
vocabWords.exampleSentence: The old lawnmower sputtered before finally starting up.

pages.label: Page 8
pages.text: Suddenly, the Wright Flyer lifted off the ground! Orville was flying! The plane stayed in the air for 12 amazing seconds and flew 120 feet before landing safely on the sand. It was the first time in history that a powered airplane had flown with a person aboard. Wilbur cheered and the five witnesses couldn't believe their eyes. The age of flight had begun!
pages.scene: The Wright Flyer airborne just a few feet off the ground with Orville piloting. Wilbur stands on the ground with his arms raised in celebration. The five witnesses point and stare in amazement. The biplane casts a shadow on the sandy ground below. This is the famous first flight moment captured in history.

vocabWords.definition_4_7: To rise up into the air from the ground.
vocabWords.definition_7_10: To move upward from a lower position, rising against the pull of gravity toward the sky.
vocabWords.exampleSentence: The helium balloon lifted slowly toward the ceiling.

vocabWords.definition_4_7: Something that uses an engine or electricity to work.
vocabWords.definition_7_10: Operated by a source of energy like fuel or electricity rather than just human strength alone.
vocabWords.exampleSentence: The powered skateboard moved faster than a regular one.

vocabWords.definition_4_7: On top of or inside a vehicle like a ship.
vocabWords.definition_7_10: Riding as a passenger on or in a vehicle such as a plane, ship, or train.
vocabWords.exampleSentence: All passengers came aboard the cruise ship before it sailed.

pages.label: Page 9
pages.text: The brothers weren't finished yet! They made three more flights that day, taking turns as pilot. Each flight went farther and stayed up longer than the one before. The fourth and final flight was the best of all - Wilbur flew for 59 seconds and covered 852 feet! But then a gust of wind flipped the plane over and damaged it. Their flying was done for the day, but they had made history.
pages.scene: Wilbur piloting the Wright Flyer on the longest flight of the day, flying higher and farther than before. The plane is more stable and confident in the air. Below, Orville and the witnesses track its progress. The ocean and dunes of Kitty Hawk stretch out in the background under a partly cloudy sky.

vocabWords.definition_4_7: Times when something flies through the air.
vocabWords.definition_7_10: Journeys made by aircraft traveling through the air from takeoff to landing.
vocabWords.exampleSentence: The airport had many flights leaving for different cities.

vocabWords.definition_4_7: To travel across a certain distance or area.
vocabWords.definition_7_10: To travel over or across a particular distance, moving from one point to another.
vocabWords.exampleSentence: The hiking trail covered five miles through the forest.

vocabWords.definition_4_7: Something that got broken or hurt and doesn't work right.
vocabWords.definition_7_10: Harmed or broken in a way that affects how well something works or looks.
vocabWords.exampleSentence: The storm damaged several houses in our neighborhood.

pages.label: Page 10
pages.text: That evening, the Wright brothers sent a telegram to their father back in Dayton, Ohio. It said they had made four successful flights and would be home for Christmas. At first, many people didn't believe that two bicycle mechanics had actually learned to fly. But Wilbur and Orville Wright had changed the world forever. Their dream of flight had come true on the sandy dunes of Kitty Hawk.
pages.scene: The Wright brothers inside a telegraph office, with Orville writing out their historic telegram while Wilbur looks on. The telegraph operator sits at his key. Through the window, the damaged Wright Flyer can be seen on the dunes. Oil lamps light the small office. The telegram paper is visible on the desk.

vocabWords.definition_4_7: A short message sent quickly over long distances.
vocabWords.definition_7_10: An old-fashioned way of sending brief written messages quickly across great distances using wires.
vocabWords.exampleSentence: Grandpa sent a telegram to announce the baby's birth.

vocabWords.definition_4_7: When something works out the way you hoped it would.
vocabWords.definition_7_10: Achieving the goal you set out to accomplish, turning out well and as planned.
vocabWords.exampleSentence: Her successful science project won first place at the fair.

vocabWords.definition_4_7: People who fix machines and know how they work.
vocabWords.definition_7_10: Workers who specialize in repairing and building machines, understanding how mechanical things operate.
vocabWords.exampleSentence: The mechanics at the garage fixed our car's engine.

comprehensionQuestions.question: Where did the Wright brothers test their flying machine?

options.text: At Kitty Hawk, North Carolina
In their bicycle shop in Ohio
On a mountain in Colorado

comprehensionQuestions.question: Why do you think the brothers chose sandy dunes for their flying tests?

options.text: The sand would make a softer landing if they crashed
The sand was easier to dig holes in
The sand looked prettier than grass

comprehensionQuestions.question: How would you feel if you were Orville during that first 12-second flight?

options.text: Excited and amazed that it actually worked
Nervous but proud to make history
Thrilled to be flying like a bird

predictionPause.question: What do you think will happen when Orville tries to fly the Wright Flyer?

options.text: The plane will lift off and fly successfully
The engine will break and the plane won't move
The plane will roll forward but stay on the ground

h1.title: The First Moon Landing
h1.topic: The Apollo 11 mission in July 1969. Neil Armstrong, Buzz Aldrin, and Michael Collins flew to the moon. Armstrong was the first person to walk on the moon, saying 'That\'s one small step for man, one giant leap for mankind.' Aldrin joined him while Collins orbited above.
h1.keyFacts: Launch date July 16 1969, landing July 20, Saturn V rocket, Sea of Tranquility landing site, planted American flag, collected moon rocks, splashdown in Pacific Ocean

pages.label: Page 1
pages.text: On July 16, 1969, three brave astronauts stood ready for an incredible journey. Neil Armstrong, Buzz Aldrin, and Michael Collins were about to fly to the Moon! They climbed into their spacecraft called Apollo 11, which sat atop a enormous rocket named Saturn V. The rocket was taller than a 36-story building and hummed with power.
pages.scene: A massive Saturn V rocket on the launch pad at Kennedy Space Center, dawn light breaking over the Florida coast. Three astronauts in white spacesuits walking toward the launch tower. Hundreds of people watching from viewing areas. The rocket gleaming white against the sky, with the American flag visible on its side.

vocabWords.definition_4_7: People who travel to space in special rockets.
vocabWords.definition_7_10: Brave explorers who train for years to travel beyond Earth's atmosphere in spacecraft.
vocabWords.exampleSentence: The astronauts on the space station can see Earth from far away.

vocabWords.definition_4_7: A special vehicle that can fly in space.
vocabWords.definition_7_10: A carefully built machine designed to carry people safely through the emptiness of space.
vocabWords.exampleSentence: The new spacecraft will carry supplies to the space station.

vocabWords.definition_4_7: Much bigger than normal things you see every day.
vocabWords.definition_7_10: So incredibly large that it's hard to believe something could be that huge.
vocabWords.exampleSentence: The enormous whale was bigger than three school buses put together.

vocabWords.definition_4_7: So amazing that it's hard to believe it's real.
vocabWords.definition_7_10: Something so wonderful and surprising that it takes your breath away with excitement.
vocabWords.exampleSentence: The magician did an incredible trick that made the rabbit disappear.

pages.label: Page 2
pages.text: The countdown began: Ten, nine, eight! Scientists and engineers held their breath. Millions of people around the world watched on television. BLAST OFF! The Saturn V rocket roared to life with a deafening sound. Flames shot downward as the spacecraft lifted higher and higher into the blue sky.
pages.scene: The moment of launch—the Saturn V rocket lifting off with massive flames and smoke billowing beneath it. The launch pad empty below. Control room technicians wearing headsets, monitoring instruments. Families watching on television sets with wood-grain cabinets, wearing 1960s clothing.

vocabWords.definition_4_7: Counting backwards from a big number to zero.
vocabWords.definition_7_10: The exciting moment when numbers are called backwards before something important happens.
vocabWords.exampleSentence: We did a countdown from ten before the New Year fireworks started.

vocabWords.definition_4_7: Smart people who build and fix complicated machines.
vocabWords.definition_7_10: Clever problem-solvers who design and create amazing things like bridges, rockets, and computers.
vocabWords.exampleSentence: The engineers built a strong bridge that cars can drive across safely.

vocabWords.definition_4_7: A sound so loud it hurts your ears.
vocabWords.definition_7_10: An incredibly loud noise that's so powerful you can barely hear anything else.
vocabWords.exampleSentence: The thunder was deafening during the big storm last night.

pages.label: Page 3
pages.text: The three astronauts felt themselves being pushed deeper into their seats. Up, up, up they went! In just minutes, Apollo eleven had left Earth's air behind. The sky turned from blue to black as they soared into space. Through their windows, Earth became smaller and smaller until it looked like a blue marble.
pages.scene: View from inside the spacecraft—three astronauts in helmets and spacesuits strapped into their seats, looking focused and calm. Large window showing Earth shrinking below them. Instrument panels with dials and switches surrounding them. Stars visible in the black space above.

vocabWords.definition_4_7: Flew up high and fast through the air.
vocabWords.definition_7_10: Rose gracefully and powerfully through the sky like a magnificent bird in flight.
vocabWords.exampleSentence: The eagle soared above the mountains looking for food.

vocabWords.definition_4_7: A small round ball that you can play with.
vocabWords.definition_7_10: A tiny sphere that's smooth and shiny, often made of glass or stone.
vocabWords.exampleSentence: I found a pretty blue marble in the playground sandbox.

vocabWords.definition_4_7: The air that goes all around our planet Earth.
vocabWords.definition_7_10: The invisible layer of gases that surrounds Earth and gives us air to breathe.
vocabWords.exampleSentence: Airplanes fly high up in Earth's atmosphere above the clouds.

pages.label: Page 4
pages.text: For three days, the astronauts traveled through space toward the Moon. Michael Collins would stay in the Command Module orbiting around the Moon. But Neil Armstrong and Buzz Aldrin would go down to the Moon's surface in a smaller spacecraft called the Lunar Module. It looked like a giant metal spider with legs!
pages.scene: The spacecraft traveling through the void of space. Earth visible in the distance as a blue sphere. The Moon ahead, getting larger—gray and cratered. Inside, the three astronauts floating in zero gravity, with food packets and water bags visible. Technical drawings of the Lunar Module shown on their instruments.

vocabWords.definition_4_7: Going around and around something in a big circle.
vocabWords.definition_7_10: Moving in a curved path that goes around and around a planet or moon.
vocabWords.exampleSentence: The space station is orbiting Earth right above our heads.

vocabWords.definition_4_7: A separate part of something bigger that does special work.
vocabWords.definition_7_10: An important section or compartment that has its own special job to do.
vocabWords.exampleSentence: Each module of the space station has different equipment inside it.

vocabWords.definition_4_7: A small creature with eight long legs that makes webs.
vocabWords.definition_7_10: An eight-legged animal that spins silk threads to catch insects for food.
vocabWords.exampleSentence: The spider made a beautiful web between two tree branches.

pages.label: Page 5
pages.text: On July 20, 1969, the great moment arrived. Neil Armstrong and Buzz Aldrin climbed into the Lunar Module and separated from Michael Collins. Down, down, down they descended toward the Moon's surface. Their hearts beat faster as they got closer and closer to the gray, dusty ground below them.
pages.scene: The Lunar Module separating from the Command Module in space. Through the window, the Moon's surface approaching—gray, barren, and covered with craters and rocks. The descent engine firing. Armstrong and Aldrin visible through the small windows, focused on their instruments. The Moon filling their entire view.

vocabWords.definition_4_7: Moved away from something to be by themselves.
vocabWords.definition_7_10: Split apart from something else to become two different things in different places.
vocabWords.exampleSentence: The baby bird separated from its nest when it learned to fly.

vocabWords.definition_4_7: Went down lower and lower from a high place.
vocabWords.definition_7_10: Moved downward slowly and carefully from somewhere high up to somewhere lower.
vocabWords.exampleSentence: The elevator descended from the top floor to the ground floor.

vocabWords.definition_4_7: Covered with tiny bits of dirt that float around.
vocabWords.definition_7_10: Having a layer of fine, powdery particles that make things look gray and dirty.
vocabWords.exampleSentence: The old book was dusty because nobody had read it for years.

pages.label: Page 6
pages.text: The Lunar Module touched down gently on the Moon in a place called the Sea of Tranquility. Though it was called a 'sea,' there was no water—just smooth, dusty rock. Neil Armstrong looked out at the strange, silent landscape. He took a deep breath and prepared to step outside.
pages.scene: The Lunar Module's footpads touching the gray, powdery Moon surface. Rocks and dust surrounding it. The horizon of the Moon visible—harsh shadows and bright sun creating stark contrasts. Inside, Armstrong at the window, hand on the door control, in his white spacesuit. His face calm and determined.

vocabWords.definition_4_7: A feeling of being very calm and peaceful inside.
vocabWords.definition_7_10: A state of perfect quietness and peace where everything feels still and serene.
vocabWords.exampleSentence: The lake had a feeling of tranquility with no waves or wind.

vocabWords.definition_4_7: All the things you can see when you look around outside.
vocabWords.definition_7_10: The view of land with all its hills, rocks, trees, and other natural features.
vocabWords.exampleSentence: The mountain landscape was covered with green trees and flowing rivers.

vocabWords.definition_4_7: In a soft and careful way that doesn't hurt.
vocabWords.definition_7_10: Moving or touching something with tender care so nothing gets damaged or broken.
vocabWords.exampleSentence: She gently petted the kitten so it wouldn't get scared.

pages.label: Page 7
pages.text: Neil Armstrong pushed open the hatch and slowly climbed down the metal ladder. One foot, then the other. His boots touched the Moon's dusty surface. With his heart pounding, Armstrong spoke words that would be remembered forever: 'That's one small step for man, one giant leap for mankind.'
pages.scene: Neil Armstrong in his white spacesuit, descending the Lunar Module's ladder. His boot extended, about to touch the fine gray dust. The stark lunar landscape in the background. Earth visible as a small blue dot in the black sky. The Sun casting sharp shadows. His gold-tinted helmet visor reflecting the barren terrain.

vocabWords.definition_4_7: A special door on a spaceship or boat.
vocabWords.definition_7_10: A heavy door or opening that can be sealed tight to keep air and water out.
vocabWords.exampleSentence: The submarine captain opened the hatch to let fresh air inside.

vocabWords.definition_4_7: Steps you can climb up or down to reach high places.
vocabWords.definition_7_10: A tool with rungs or steps that helps people safely climb up or down.
vocabWords.exampleSentence: Dad used a ladder to hang Christmas lights on our house.

vocabWords.definition_4_7: All the people who live anywhere on planet Earth.
vocabWords.definition_7_10: Every single human being that has ever lived or will live on our world.
vocabWords.exampleSentence: The invention of medicine has helped mankind live longer, healthier lives.

pages.label: Page 8
pages.text: Armstrong walked slowly across the Moon's surface, bouncing with each step in the low gravity. Every movement was careful and deliberate. Soon, Buzz Aldrin joined him on the Moon. The two explorers walked together on this distant world, amazed by the gray rocks and the blackness of space all around them.
pages.scene: Two astronauts in white spacesuits on the gray lunar surface, moving in exaggerated bouncing motions. The Lunar Module behind them with its ladder and landing gear. Rocks and dust in various shades of gray. Earth visible in the black sky. Their long shadows cast by the bright Sun. Scientific instruments and equipment visible nearby.

vocabWords.definition_4_7: Moving up and down like a ball that hits the ground.
vocabWords.definition_7_10: Jumping or springing back repeatedly when something touches a surface and rebounds.
vocabWords.exampleSentence: The basketball kept bouncing higher and higher on the playground.

vocabWords.definition_4_7: The force that pulls things down toward the ground.
vocabWords.definition_7_10: An invisible power that makes objects fall down instead of floating in the air.
vocabWords.exampleSentence: Gravity is why your toy falls down when you drop it.

vocabWords.definition_4_7: Done slowly and carefully with lots of thinking first.
vocabWords.definition_7_10: Moving or acting with careful thought and purpose, not rushing or being careless.
vocabWords.exampleSentence: She made a deliberate choice to save her allowance for something special.

pages.label: Page 9
pages.text: Armstrong and Aldrin had important work to do on the Moon. They planted an American flag on the surface, hammering the pole into the hard ground. They also collected many Moon rocks to bring back to Earth so scientists could study them. Everything they did was being watched by millions of people on television back home.
pages.scene: Armstrong and Aldrin working on the Moon's surface. One astronaut holding a pole while the other uses a hammer to drive the American flag into the ground. Rocks being collected and placed in sample bags. Scientific instruments set up around them. Earth visible overhead. The Lunar Module in the background. Close-up of the American flag planted on the gray surface.

vocabWords.definition_4_7: People who go to new places to discover new things.
vocabWords.definition_7_10: Brave adventurers who travel to unknown places to learn about the world.
vocabWords.exampleSentence: The explorers sailed across the ocean to find new lands.

vocabWords.definition_4_7: Put something in the ground so it will stay there.
vocabWords.definition_7_10: Placed something firmly into soil or ground so it won't fall over or move.
vocabWords.exampleSentence: We planted tomato seeds in our garden last spring.

vocabWords.definition_4_7: Hitting something hard many times with a heavy tool.
vocabWords.definition_7_10: Using a hammer to pound or drive something into place with repeated strong strikes.
vocabWords.exampleSentence: Dad was hammering nails into the wood to build a birdhouse.

pages.label: Page 10
pages.text: After two and a half hours on the Moon, Armstrong and Aldrin returned to the Lunar Module. They blasted off from the Moon's surface to rejoin Michael Collins in orbit. The journey home began! Three days later, on July 24, 1969, Apollo 11 splashed down safely in the Pacific Ocean.
pages.scene: The Lunar Module ascending from the Moon's surface, leaving the flag behind. The Moon receding below. In space, the Lunar Module approaching the Command Module. The three spacecraft docking. Later, the Command Module descending toward Earth with parachutes deployed. The Pacific Ocean below, with a Navy ship waiting nearby.

vocabWords.definition_4_7: Shot up into the air with great force and power.
vocabWords.definition_7_10: Launched upward with tremendous speed and energy like a rocket taking off.
vocabWords.exampleSentence: The firework blasted high into the night sky with bright colors.

vocabWords.definition_4_7: To go back and be together with someone again.
vocabWords.definition_7_10: To meet up again with people after being separated from them for a while.
vocabWords.exampleSentence: After lunch, the students will rejoin their class for the afternoon lessons.

vocabWords.definition_4_7: The path something takes as it goes around a planet.
vocabWords.definition_7_10: The curved pathway that objects follow as they circle around a larger body in space.
vocabWords.exampleSentence: It takes one year for Earth to complete its orbit around the Sun.

pages.label: Page 11
pages.text: When the astronauts returned to Earth, the world celebrated! Neil Armstrong, Buzz Aldrin, and Michael Collins had accomplished something amazing. They had traveled to the Moon and back, proving that humans could explore space. The Moon rocks they brought home helped scientists learn about our solar system for many years to come.
pages.scene: A Navy ship recovering the Apollo 11 Command Module from the Pacific Ocean. Astronauts being lifted to safety by helicopter. Crowds on beaches and around television sets celebrating. News broadcasts showing the astronauts waving. Ticker-tape parades in cities. Scientists examining Moon rocks in laboratories. The historic moment preserved in photographs.

vocabWords.definition_4_7: Had a happy party because something wonderful happened.
vocabWords.definition_7_10: Showed joy and excitement with special activities to honor an important achievement.
vocabWords.exampleSentence: We celebrated my birthday with cake, balloons, and lots of friends.

vocabWords.definition_4_7: Finished doing something very difficult and important successfully.
vocabWords.definition_7_10: Completed a challenging task or goal that required great skill, effort, and determination.
vocabWords.exampleSentence: She accomplished her dream of learning to ride a bicycle without training wheels.

vocabWords.definition_4_7: The Sun and all the planets that go around it.
vocabWords.definition_7_10: Our cosmic neighborhood with the Sun at the center and planets orbiting around it.
vocabWords.exampleSentence: Earth is the third planet from the Sun in our solar system.

comprehensionQuestions.question: Who were the three astronauts that went to the Moon?

options.text: Neil Armstrong, Buzz Aldrin, and Michael Collins
John Glenn, Alan Shepard, and Sally Ride
Neil Armstrong, John Glenn, and Buzz Aldrin

comprehensionQuestions.question: Why do you think the astronauts bounced when they walked on the Moon?

options.text: Because the Moon has less gravity than Earth
Because their space suits were too big
Because the Moon's ground was like a trampoline

comprehensionQuestions.question: How do you think you would feel if you were the first person to step on the Moon?

options.text: Scared and nervous about being so far from home
Excited and proud to do something no one had ever done
Amazed and wonder-struck by the strange, silent world

predictionPause.question: What do you think will happen when Neil and Buzz try to land on the Moon?

options.text: They will land safely and become the first people on the Moon
They will have trouble landing and need to try again
They will decide it's too scary and go back to Michael

h7.title: Leonardo da Vinci's Workshop
h7.topic: Leonardo da Vinci (1452-1519), the Italian Renaissance genius who was a painter, inventor, scientist, and engineer. He painted the Mona Lisa and The Last Supper. He designed flying machines, tanks, and bridges centuries before they existed. He filled notebooks with drawings and mirror writing.
h7.keyFacts: Born 1452 in Vinci Italy, painted Mona Lisa (now in the Louvre) and The Last Supper, designed helicopter-like flying machine and parachute, studied human anatomy through dissections, wrote backwards in mirror script, worked for the Duke of Milan and King of France, died 1519 in France, true Renaissance Man

pages.label: Page 1: A Boy in Vinci
pages.text: In the year 1452, a baby boy was born in the small town of Vinci, near Florence, Italy. His name was Leonardo. His father was a lawyer, but Leonardo spent his days outdoors, watching birds soar through the sky and studying how water flowed in streams. He filled scraps of paper with drawings of everything he saw. Young Leonardo was curious about everything in the world around him—the clouds, the flowers, even the tiny insects crawling on leaves.
pages.scene: A sunlit Tuscan hillside town with stone buildings and terracotta roofs in the 1450s. A young boy with dark hair sits on a grassy slope, sketching birds with a stick of charcoal on parchment. Rolling green hills and cypress trees surround the scene. The light is warm and golden, capturing the Renaissance Italian countryside.

vocabWords.definition_4_7: Someone who helps people with law problems.
vocabWords.definition_7_10: A person who studies laws and helps people solve legal problems in court.
vocabWords.exampleSentence: The lawyer helped the family understand the contract.

vocabWords.definition_4_7: Wanting to learn and know about things.
vocabWords.definition_7_10: Having a strong desire to learn about new things and ask questions.
vocabWords.exampleSentence: Maya was curious about how computers work.

vocabWords.definition_4_7: Small pieces of leftover paper or material.
vocabWords.definition_7_10: Tiny bits and pieces of something that are left over after using most of it.
vocabWords.exampleSentence: She saved fabric scraps to make a quilt.

pages.label: Page 2: The Apprentice
pages.text: When Leonardo was about fourteen years old, his father arranged for him to learn painting in Florence, the great art city of Italy. Leonardo became an apprentice to Andrea del Verrocchio, a famous artist with a busy workshop. There, Leonardo mixed paints, stretched canvas, and watched carefully as Master Verrocchio created beautiful works. The workshop was filled with the smell of linseed oil and ground pigments. Leonardo learned secrets of painting that few people knew.
pages.scene: A bustling Renaissance workshop in Florence, circa 1466. High ceilings with wooden beams, large windows letting in natural light. Andrea del Verrocchio, an older bearded man in period clothing, demonstrates painting technique to young Leonardo, who watches intently. Shelves hold jars of pigments in rich colors—lapis lazuli blue, vermillion red, gold leaf. Paintings and sketches line the walls. Other apprentices work at easels in the background.

vocabWords.definition_4_7: Someone who learns a job by helping an expert.
vocabWords.definition_7_10: A young person who learns skills by working alongside and helping a master craftsperson.
vocabWords.exampleSentence: The apprentice baker learned to make bread from the master chef.

vocabWords.definition_4_7: Colored powders used to make paint and dyes.
vocabWords.definition_7_10: Natural or artificial colored substances that are ground into powder to create paints and inks.
vocabWords.exampleSentence: The artist mixed blue pigments with oil to create ocean colors.

vocabWords.definition_4_7: Seeds from flax plants used to make oil.
vocabWords.definition_7_10: Small seeds from the flax plant that are pressed to make oil for painting and other uses.
vocabWords.exampleSentence: The carpenter rubbed linseed oil into the wooden table.

pages.label: Page 3: The Secret Script
pages.text: As Leonardo grew older, he filled thousands of pages with notes and sketches. But Leonardo wrote in a mysterious way—he wrote backwards, from right to left, in what we now call mirror writing. To read his words, you had to hold the page up to a mirror! Some people thought he was hiding secrets, but Leonardo probably just enjoyed the challenge. His mirror writing meant that only truly curious people would bother to decode his brilliant ideas.
pages.scene: Leonardo's desk, scattered with papers, quills, and ink bottles. Candlelight illuminates a page showing backwards mirror writing and detailed sketches of anatomical drawings, flying machines, and mechanical designs. Leonardo, now a young man in his twenties with sharp, intelligent eyes and period clothing, writes with concentration. A mirror sits on the desk reflecting the writing. Shelves behind him hold leather-bound books and scrolls.

vocabWords.definition_4_7: Strange and hard to understand or explain.
vocabWords.definition_7_10: Something puzzling and secretive that makes people wonder and ask questions about it.
vocabWords.exampleSentence: The mysterious footprints in the snow led to the forest.

vocabWords.definition_4_7: Quick drawings that show the main parts of something.
vocabWords.definition_7_10: Simple drawings made quickly to capture the basic shape and details of an object or idea.
vocabWords.exampleSentence: The architect drew sketches of the new playground design.

vocabWords.definition_4_7: To figure out the meaning of a secret message.
vocabWords.definition_7_10: To solve a puzzle or mystery by translating hidden information into something understandable.
vocabWords.exampleSentence: The spy needed to decode the secret message from headquarters.

pages.label: Page 4: Duke Ludovico's Workshop
pages.text: In 1482, Leonardo moved to Milan, where he worked for the powerful Duke Ludovico Sforza. The Duke gave Leonardo his own workshop and paid him to paint, design weapons, build machines, and plan buildings. For nearly twenty years, Milan became Leonardo's home. He created paintings that amazed everyone who saw them. But Leonardo was thinking about much more than art. He was imagining flying machines, studying how human bodies worked, and dreaming up inventions that would not be built for hundreds of years.
pages.scene: A grand Renaissance workshop in Milan, circa 1485. Soaring arched ceilings and tall windows showing the Duomo cathedral of Milan in the background. Leonardo, now a bearded man in his thirties wearing rich Renaissance clothes, stands before a large canvas. Around the workshop are half-finished paintings, wooden models of machines, anatomical drawings, and blueprints. The Duke Ludovico, wearing noble attire, examines Leonardo's work. Assistants work at various stations. The room is filled with artistic and scientific tools.
sounds: the distant sound of the city

vocabWords.definition_4_7: A powerful nobleman who rules over land and people.
vocabWords.definition_7_10: A high-ranking noble person who has authority over a region and reports to a king or queen.
vocabWords.exampleSentence: The Duke invited everyone in the kingdom to a grand feast.

vocabWords.definition_4_7: Tools made for fighting or protecting in battles.
vocabWords.definition_7_10: Objects designed for fighting, hunting, or defense that can cause harm to enemies in warfare.
vocabWords.exampleSentence: The museum displayed ancient weapons from medieval times.

vocabWords.definition_4_7: New things that someone creates or makes for the first time.
vocabWords.definition_7_10: Original creations or devices that solve problems or make life easier in ways never done before.
vocabWords.exampleSentence: The telephone was one of the most important inventions in history.

pages.label: Page 5: The Flying Machine
pages.text: Leonardo watched birds more carefully than anyone had ever done before. He sketched their wings, studying how they caught the wind and air currents. He drew a machine with large wings made of wood and cloth—a device that could carry a person through the sky! He called it the ornithopter. Centuries later, people would invent airplanes, but Leonardo had imagined them long before. His drawings showed the world what was possible if we dared to dream.
pages.scene: Leonardo's workshop filled with large mechanical drawings and wooden models. In the foreground, detailed sketches of bird wings and a large flying machine with bat-like wings made of wood and canvas. Leonardo, holding a sketch, stands beside an assistant examining a wooden frame model of the ornithopter. Hanging from the ceiling are anatomical drawings of bird wings. Papers with mirror writing and precise engineering drawings cover every surface. Afternoon light streams through tall windows.

vocabWords.definition_4_7: A flying machine with wings that flap like a bird's.
vocabWords.definition_7_10: An aircraft designed to fly by flapping its wings up and down like a real bird does.
vocabWords.exampleSentence: Scientists today are still trying to build a working ornithopter.

vocabWords.definition_4_7: Moving streams of air or water flowing in one direction.
vocabWords.definition_7_10: Flowing movements of air, water, or electricity that travel steadily in a particular direction.
vocabWords.exampleSentence: The sailboat used ocean currents to move faster across the water.

vocabWords.definition_4_7: Periods of time that last for one hundred years each.
vocabWords.definition_7_10: Very long time periods, each lasting exactly one hundred years, used to measure history.
vocabWords.exampleSentence: Centuries ago, people traveled by horse instead of cars.

pages.label: Page 6: Understanding the Body
pages.text: To paint and draw the human body perfectly, Leonardo did something very unusual for his time—he studied dead bodies. In secret, he performed dissections, carefully cutting open corpses to see the muscles, bones, and organs inside. He drew what he discovered with incredible accuracy, sometimes dissecting different parts over and over. These drawings were the most detailed anatomical studies anyone had ever made. Leonardo's curiosity about how bodies worked helped him create art that looked more alive and real than anyone else's.
pages.scene: Leonardo's private study, dimly lit by candlelight. A respectful, solemn scene showing Leonardo in his workshop, examining anatomical drawings spread across a wooden table. Detailed, historically accurate medical drawings visible on papers—muscles, organs, the human skeleton. Books on anatomy nearby. The scene is serious and scientific, not graphic. Leonardo is focused and thoughtful. Medical instruments and measuring tools sit on the table. The atmosphere is one of quiet scientific investigation.

vocabWords.definition_4_7: Carefully cutting open bodies to study what's inside them.
vocabWords.definition_7_10: The scientific process of cutting apart dead animals or people to learn about body parts and how they work.
vocabWords.exampleSentence: Medical students learn about the heart through careful dissections.

vocabWords.definition_4_7: Dead bodies of people or animals.
vocabWords.definition_7_10: The remains of people or animals after they have died and are no longer alive.
vocabWords.exampleSentence: Archaeologists sometimes find ancient corpses buried in old tombs.

vocabWords.definition_4_7: Having to do with the parts inside bodies and how they work.
vocabWords.definition_7_10: Related to the structure and arrangement of body parts like bones, muscles, and organs.
vocabWords.exampleSentence: The doctor used an anatomical chart to explain how lungs function.

pages.label: Page 7: The Last Supper
pages.text: In 1495, the Duke asked Leonardo to paint a large picture on the wall of a church dining hall in Milan. The painting showed Jesus's last meal with his twelve followers, called the Last Supper. Leonardo spent three years painting it, thinking carefully about every detail. The finished painting was enormous—nearly thirty feet wide! It showed Jesus and his apostles in the exact moment when Jesus says, 'One of you will betray me.' Every face showed a different emotion. It became one of the most famous paintings in the world.
pages.scene: The refectory (dining hall) of Santa Maria delle Grazie in Milan. A massive fresco covers one wall showing Jesus at the center of a long table with twelve apostles around him. The scene captures dramatic emotion—shock, confusion, loyalty, and betrayal on different faces. The composition is perfectly balanced with Renaissance perspective. Leonardo stands on a scaffold with brushes, studying his work. The hall has Renaissance arched windows and stone architecture. Golden afternoon light illuminates the massive painting.

vocabWords.definition_4_7: The twelve special friends and followers of Jesus.
vocabWords.definition_7_10: The group of twelve men who were Jesus's closest disciples and helped spread his teachings.
vocabWords.exampleSentence: The apostles traveled to many cities to share Jesus's message.

vocabWords.definition_4_7: To hurt someone who trusts you by helping their enemies.
vocabWords.definition_7_10: To break someone's trust by acting against them or revealing their secrets to enemies.
vocabWords.exampleSentence: She felt hurt when her best friend decided to betray her secret.

vocabWords.definition_4_7: Extremely big and much larger than normal size.
vocabWords.definition_7_10: Incredibly huge and impressive in size, much bigger than anything you would normally see.
vocabWords.exampleSentence: The enormous whale was longer than three school buses.

pages.label: Page 8: The Mona Lisa's Smile
pages.text: Around the year 1503, Leonardo began painting a portrait of a woman named Lisa del Giocondo. He worked on the painting for years, taking it with him when he traveled. The painting shows a woman with a mysterious, gentle smile. Her eyes seem to follow you around the room. Leonardo used a special technique called sfumato, which means blending colors so smoothly that you can barely see the brush strokes. Today, this painting, called the Mona Lisa, hangs in the famous Louvre museum in Paris. Millions of people visit every year to see that mysterious smile.
pages.scene: Leonardo's private studio. He sits before a small wooden panel, carefully painting fine details of a woman's face with a subtle, enigmatic smile. The woman, Lisa del Giocondo, sits in period dress from early 1500s Italy. Around them are sketches, paint jars, and brushes. A window shows an Italian Renaissance landscape with winding paths and soft, misty mountains in the background—the same landscape visible in the painting. The light is soft and diffused. Leonardo's expression shows intense concentration and care.
sounds: the scratch of quill on paper

vocabWords.definition_4_7: A painting or drawing that shows a person's face.
vocabWords.definition_7_10: An artwork that captures someone's appearance, especially focusing on their face and expression.
vocabWords.exampleSentence: Grandma hung a portrait of her parents above the fireplace.

vocabWords.definition_4_7: A painting technique that blends colors very smoothly together.
vocabWords.definition_7_10: An artistic method of blending paint colors so softly that you cannot see where one ends and another begins.
vocabWords.exampleSentence: The artist used sfumato to make the sunset look realistic.

vocabWords.definition_4_7: A special way of doing something that requires skill.
vocabWords.definition_7_10: A particular method or approach for accomplishing a task that requires practice and expertise.
vocabWords.exampleSentence: The chef taught us a new technique for chopping vegetables safely.

pages.label: Page 9: Dreams of War and Peace
pages.text: Leonardo filled his notebooks with designs for many inventions. He sketched a machine that looked like an armored tank, protected by metal shields and armed with weapons. He designed a submarine-like vessel that could travel underwater. He drew a parachute, a bridge that could span great distances, and a screw that could lift water. Though Leonardo worked for powerful dukes and kings, he truly hated war. In his heart, he dreamed of machines that would help people, not hurt them. His inventions were centuries ahead of their time.
pages.scene: Leonardo's workshop overflowing with architectural and mechanical blueprints. Pages spread across tables show detailed drawings of a tank-like war machine, a flying screw (helicopter design), a parachute, arched bridges, and hydraulic systems. All drawings show Leonardo's characteristic precise, detailed style with measurements and notes in mirror writing. The workshop has high shelves packed with books, scrolls, models, and scientific instruments. A world globe, compass, and measuring tools are visible. Leonardo stands studying multiple drawings, appearing both inspired and troubled.

vocabWords.definition_4_7: Protected by strong metal covering that blocks weapons.
vocabWords.definition_7_10: Covered with hard metal plates or shields designed to protect against attacks and damage.
vocabWords.exampleSentence: The armored truck safely carried money to the bank.

vocabWords.definition_4_7: A boat that can travel underwater beneath the surface.
vocabWords.definition_7_10: A watercraft designed to operate underwater by diving below the ocean's surface and traveling beneath waves.
vocabWords.exampleSentence: The submarine explored the deep ocean floor looking for treasure.

vocabWords.definition_4_7: A large cloth that opens like an umbrella to slow falling.
vocabWords.definition_7_10: A device made of fabric that opens wide to create air resistance and slow down someone falling from high places.
vocabWords.exampleSentence: The skydiver pulled the cord and her parachute opened above the clouds.

pages.label: Page 10: The King of France
pages.text: In 1516, Leonardo was invited to France by King Francis I. The French king greatly admired Leonardo's genius and gave him a comfortable home called the Manor of Cloux. Leonardo brought the Mona Lisa painting with him to France. During these final years, Leonardo continued sketching and thinking about his ideas, though he suffered from illness. He filled notebooks with plans for new inventions and wrote thousands of notes about art, science, and nature. On May 2, 1519, Leonardo died in France at the age of sixty-seven, leaving behind notebooks filled with brilliant ideas for future generations to discover.
pages.scene: The Manor of Cloux in France, a graceful Renaissance château. An elderly Leonardo, with long white beard and period clothing, sits in a comfortable study surrounded by his lifetime of notebooks and sketches. King Francis I, wearing royal robes, visits him warmly. Sunlight streams through tall windows showing the French countryside. The Mona Lisa painting is visible on an easel. Books, papers, and drawings cover every surface. The atmosphere is one of respect and quiet dignity. Leonardo appears thoughtful and at peace.

vocabWords.definition_4_7: Looked up to someone and thought they were wonderful.
vocabWords.definition_7_10: Felt respect and appreciation for someone because of their talents, achievements, or good qualities.
vocabWords.exampleSentence: Everyone admired the firefighter for her brave rescue.

vocabWords.definition_4_7: Someone who is extremely smart and talented at many things.
vocabWords.definition_7_10: A person with exceptional intelligence and creative abilities that far exceed those of ordinary people.
vocabWords.exampleSentence: Mozart was a musical genius who composed beautiful songs as a child.

vocabWords.definition_4_7: Groups of people born around the same time period.
vocabWords.definition_7_10: People who are born and live during the same time period, often including parents, children, and grandchildren.
vocabWords.exampleSentence: Three generations of the family gathered for the reunion.

pages.label: Page 11: His Legacy Lives On
pages.text: Leonardo left behind thousands of notebook pages filled with sketches and ideas. Modern scientists and engineers have studied his designs and discovered how brilliant he was. Many of his ideas eventually became real inventions—flying machines, tanks, submarines, and parachutes. Leonardo proved that one person's curiosity and imagination could change the world. He was an artist, inventor, scientist, engineer, and dreamer all at once. Today, Leonardo da Vinci is remembered as one of history's greatest minds, a true Renaissance Man who saw the world in ways no one else could imagine.
pages.scene: A museum exhibition showcasing Leonardo's legacy. Glass cases display original Renaissance notebooks with mirror writing and sketches. On walls, reproductions of the Mona Lisa and The Last Supper are displayed. Modern inventions inspired by his designs are shown—early airplane prototypes, engineering models, scientific instruments. A diverse group of modern children and adults examine the displays with wonder and fascination. Books about Leonardo are stacked nearby. The scene bridges past and present, showing how his ideas continue to inspire. Soft museum lighting illuminates everything.

vocabWords.definition_4_7: People who design and build machines, roads, and buildings.
vocabWords.definition_7_10: Professionals who use science and math to create, design, and construct useful structures and devices.
vocabWords.exampleSentence: Engineers built a strong bridge that could hold heavy trucks.

vocabWords.definition_4_7: A time when art, science, and learning grew quickly in Europe.
vocabWords.definition_7_10: A historical period of great cultural growth when people rediscovered art, science, and learning after the Middle Ages.
vocabWords.exampleSentence: Renaissance artists created some of the most beautiful paintings in history.

vocabWords.definition_4_7: The ability to think of new ideas and picture things in your mind.
vocabWords.definition_7_10: The creative power to form mental images and ideas that don't exist yet in the real world.
vocabWords.exampleSentence: Her imagination helped her write amazing stories about talking animals.

comprehensionQuestions.question: What special way did Leonardo write his notes and ideas?

options.text: He wrote backwards from right to left
He only wrote in pictures and drawings
He wrote with invisible ink

comprehensionQuestions.question: Why do you think Leonardo studied dead bodies to improve his art?

options.text: He wanted to understand how muscles and bones worked
He was trying to bring people back to life
He needed bodies to pose for his paintings

comprehensionQuestions.question: How does Leonardo's curiosity remind you of yourself or someone you know?

options.text: I love asking questions about how things work
I like to draw and create new inventions
I enjoy exploring nature and watching animals

predictionPause.question: Leonardo has been studying bodies and painting amazing art. What do you think he'll create next?

options.text: A famous painting that everyone will remember
New flying machines based on his bird studies
Inventions that help people in their daily lives
